name: Indecomposable Group
statement: A group G is indecomposable when G ≠ 1 and G = A ⊕ B implies A = 1 or B = 1.

name: Dedekind domain
statement: A Dedekind domain is an integral domain that is not a field, Noetherian, integrally closed in its field of fractions, and every nonzero prime ideal is maximal.
original_proof: This is a definition; no proof is required. Equivalent characterizations include: every nonzero proper ideal factors uniquely as a product of prime ideals.

name: Syzygy Module
statement: For a set of polynomials f₁,...,f_t in a ring R, the syzygy module Syz(f₁,...,f_t) is the set of all (h₁,...,h_t) ∈ R^t such that h₁f₁ + ... + h_t f_t = 0.
original_proof: This is a definition; no proof is required.

name: Ramification Degree at a Point
statement: The ramification degree of a nonconstant morphism h : C → C' at a point P ∈ C is e_P(h) = ν_P(t' ∘ h), where t' is a uniformizer at h(P) and ν_P is the discrete valuation on the function field of C.
original_proof: Definition; no proof.

name: Description of Subgroup Generated by a Set
statement: If G is a group and X is a nonempty subset of G, then the subgroup ⟨X⟩ generated by X consists of all finite products a_1^{n_1} a_2^{n_2} ... a_t^{n_t} (a_i ∈ X; n_i ∈ ℤ). In particular, for every a ∈ G, ⟨a⟩ = {a^n | n ∈ ℤ}.
gapfill_proof:
1. Define the set H = {a_1^{n_1} a_2^{n_2} ... a_t^{n_t} : t ∈ ℕ, a_i ∈ X, n_i ∈ ℤ}, where the empty product (t=0) is the identity element e of G.

2. Since X is nonempty, choose x ∈ X. The element x^0 = e belongs to H (taking t=0 in the definition of H, or equivalently t=1, a_1=x, n_1=0). Hence H ≠ ∅.

3. Let u = a_1^{n_1} ... a_r^{n_r} ∈ H and v = b_1^{m_1} ... b_s^{m_s} ∈ H. Concatenating these finite sequences yields uv = a_1^{n_1} ... a_r^{n_r} b_1^{m_1} ... b_s^{m_s}, which is again a finite product of integer powers of elements of X. Hence uv ∈ H (closure under the group operation).

4. Let u = a_1^{n_1} ... a_t^{n_t} ∈ H. By the group axiom of inverses applied inductively, (xy)^{-1} = y^{-1}x^{-1} for all x, y ∈ G. Applying this repeatedly gives u^{-1} = (a_t^{n_t})^{-1} ... (a_1^{n_1})^{-1}. By the definition of integer powers in a group, (a_i^{n_i})^{-1} = a_i^{-n_i}. Thus u^{-1} = a_t^{-n_t} ... a_1^{-n_1}, which is a finite product of integer powers of elements of X. Hence u^{-1} ∈ H (closure under inverses).

5. By the Two-Step Subgroup Test (Axiom/Theorem: a nonempty subset of a group that is closed under the group operation and closed under inverses is a subgroup), H ≤ G.

6. For each x ∈ X, write x = x^1, a finite product of length 1. Thus x ∈ H for all x ∈ X. Therefore X ⊆ H.

7. Let K be an arbitrary subgroup of G satisfying X ⊆ K.

8. By the definition of a subgroup, K is closed under the binary operation of G. By induction on the number of factors, K contains every finite product of elements taken from K.

9. By the definition of a subgroup, K is closed under inverses. Combined with closure under the operation, K contains every integer power of any element k ∈ K: for n > 0, k^n = k·k·...·k (n factors) ∈ K; k^0 = e ∈ K because K, as a subgroup, contains the identity; for n < 0, k^{-|n|} = (k^{|n|})^{-1} ∈ K by closure under inverses.

10. From steps 8 and 9, every element of H — which is a finite product of integer powers of elements from X ⊆ K — belongs to K. Therefore H ⊆ K.

11. By the definition of the subgroup generated by a subset (Definition: ⟨X⟩ = ⋂_{K ≤ G, X ⊆ K} K, the intersection of all subgroups of G that contain X), ⟨X⟩ is the smallest subgroup containing X in the sense that ⟨X⟩ ⊆ K for every subgroup K with X ⊆ K.

12. The intersection of any nonempty collection of subgroups of a group is itself a subgroup (Theorem: Intersection of Subgroups). Since H is a subgroup of G containing X (by steps 5 and 6), H belongs to the collection over which ⟨X⟩ is defined as an intersection.

13. By the definition of intersection, ⟨X⟩ ⊆ H (every element common to all subgroups in the collection is, in particular, an element of H).

14. By step 10, taking K = ⟨X⟩ (which is a subgroup of G containing X by definition), we obtain H ⊆ ⟨X⟩.

15. From steps 13 and 14, ⟨X⟩ ⊆ H and H ⊆ ⟨X⟩. By the axiom of extensionality, H = ⟨X⟩.

16. For the special case X = {a} for a single element a ∈ G: every element of H is of the form a^{n_1} a^{n_2} ... a^{n_t}. By associativity of the group operation repeatedly applied, and by the law of exponents a^{i} a^{j} = a^{i+j} (which follows from the definition of integer powers via repeated application of the group operation for positive exponents, and the definitions a^0 = e and a^{-n} = (a^{-1})^n for negative exponents), the product collapses to a^{n_1 + n_2 + ... + n_t}, a single integer power of a. Hence ⟨a⟩ = {a^n : n ∈ ℤ}. ∎
original_proof: Let H be the set of all such finite products. Show H is a subgroup of G containing X and contained in every subgroup that contains X. Therefore H ≤ ⟨X⟩ ≤ H, yielding equality.

name: K-algebra (Algebra over K)
statement: Let K be a commutative ring with identity. A K-algebra (or algebra over K) A is a ring A such that: (i) (A,+) is a unitary (left) K-module; (ii) k(ab) = (ka)b = a(kb) for all k ∈ K and a,b ∈ A. A K-algebra A which, as a ring, is a division ring, is called a division algebra. An algebra over a field K that is finite dimensional as a vector space over K is called a finite dimensional algebra over K.
original_proof: None (definition).

name: Definition of a Homomorphism of Semigroups
statement: Let G and H be semigroups. A function f: G -> H that preserves the given algebraic structure (i.e., f(ab) = f(a)f(b) for all a,b in G) is called a homomorphism.
original_proof: None (definition)

name: Description of Principal Ideal
statement: Let R be a ring and a ∈ R. The principal ideal (a) consists of all finite sums of elements of the forms na (n ∈ Z), ra, as, and r a s (r,s ∈ R).
gapfill_proof:
1. Define S = { ∑ᵢ₌₁ᵏ gᵢ | k ∈ ℕ, each gᵢ is of one of the four forms: na (n ∈ ℤ), ra (r ∈ R), as (s ∈ R), or ras (r,s ∈ R) }. This S is the candidate set claimed to equal the principal ideal (a).

2. Verify 0 ∈ S. Take n = 0 in the form na: 0·a = 0 is a term of type na, so the singleton sum consisting of 0·a belongs to S. Hence S is nonempty. (Definition of integer multiple in a ring: 0·x = 0 for all x ∈ R.)

3. Verify closure under addition (S + S ⊆ S). Let x = ∑ᵢ gᵢ and y = ∑ⱼ hⱼ be two elements of S, each a finite sum of generators. Then x + y = (∑ᵢ gᵢ) + (∑ⱼ hⱼ) is a concatenation of the two finite sums, which is again a finite sum of generators. Hence x + y ∈ S. This holds by the definition of S as the set of all finite sums of generators, without any bound on the number of summands.

4. Verify closure under additive inverses (−S ⊆ S). Let x = ∑ᵢ gᵢ ∈ S. Then −x = ∑ᵢ (−gᵢ). For each generator type:
   - If gᵢ = na, then −gᵢ = −(na) = (−n)a, which is of type na (with integer coefficient −n ∈ ℤ).
   - If gᵢ = ra, then −gᵢ = −(ra) = (−r)a, which is of type ra (with ring element −r ∈ R).
   - If gᵢ = as, then −gᵢ = −(as) = a(−s), which is of type as (with ring element −s ∈ R).
   - If gᵢ = ras, then −gᵢ = −(ras) = (−r)as, which is of type ras (with ring element −r ∈ R replacing r).
   Each −gᵢ is thus a generator, so ∑ᵢ (−gᵢ) is a finite sum of generators, hence −x ∈ S. (Ring axiom: (−x)y = −(xy) = x(−y) for all x,y ∈ R.)

5. From steps 2–4, (S, +) is a subgroup of the additive group (R, +), by the Subgroup Criterion: a nonempty subset closed under subtraction is a subgroup. (Closure under subtraction S − S ⊆ S follows from closure under addition and additive inverses: for x,y ∈ S, x − y = x + (−y) ∈ S.)

6. Verify S is closed under left multiplication by arbitrary ring elements (R·S ⊆ S). Let r' ∈ R and let x = ∑ᵢ gᵢ ∈ S. Then r'x = r'(∑ᵢ gᵢ) = ∑ᵢ r'gᵢ, by distributivity in the ring R. Examine r'gᵢ for each generator type:
   - If gᵢ = na: r'(na) = r'(a + a + ... + a) = r'a + r'a + ... + r'a (n terms), where the equality holds by distributivity and the definition of integer multiples. Each term r'a is of type ra. Thus r'gᵢ is a finite sum (with |n| terms) of generators of type ra.
   - If gᵢ = ra: r'(ra) = (r'r)a, which is of type ra (with ring element r'r ∈ R).
   - If gᵢ = as: r'(as) = (r'a)s, which is of type ras (with ring elements r' ∈ R and s ∈ R).
   - If gᵢ = ras: r'(ras) = (r'r)as, which is of type ras (with ring elements r'r ∈ R and s ∈ R).
   In every case, r'gᵢ is either a generator or a finite sum of generators. Summing over all i yields a finite sum of generators, so r'x ∈ S.

7. Verify S is closed under right multiplication by arbitrary ring elements (S·R ⊆ S). Let s' ∈ R and let x = ∑ᵢ gᵢ ∈ S. Then xs' = (∑ᵢ gᵢ)s' = ∑ᵢ gᵢs', by distributivity. Examine gᵢs' for each generator type:
   - If gᵢ = na: (na)s' = n(as'). Each term as' is of type as. Thus gᵢs' is a finite sum (with |n| terms) of generators of type as.
   - If gᵢ = ra: (ra)s' = r(as'), which is of type ras (with ring elements r ∈ R and s' ∈ R).
   - If gᵢ = as: (as)s' = a(ss'), which is of type as (with ring element ss' ∈ R).
   - If gᵢ = ras: (ras)s' = ra(ss'), which is of type ras (with ring elements r ∈ R and ss' ∈ R).
   Every gᵢs' is a generator or a finite sum of generators, so xs' ∈ S.

8. Conclude: S is an ideal of R. By definition, an ideal is an additive subgroup of R that is closed under left and right multiplication by arbitrary elements of R. Step 5 establishes that S is an additive subgroup, and Steps 6–7 establish the two-sided absorption property.

9. Show a ∈ S. Take the generator g₁ = 1·a (with n = 1 in the form na). Then the singleton sum a = 1·a belongs to S, by the definition of S as the set of all finite sums of generators. (In any ring, 1·a = a by the definition of the ℤ-module action.)

10. Show S ⊆ J for every ideal J of R that contains a. Let J be any ideal of R with a ∈ J.
    - For any n ∈ ℤ, na = a + a + ... + a (|n| terms with appropriate sign) belongs to J, because J is an additive subgroup of R (closed under repeated addition and additive inverses).
    - For any r ∈ R, ra ∈ J, because J is a left ideal: it absorbs left multiplication by arbitrary ring elements, and a ∈ J.
    - For any s ∈ R, as ∈ J, because J is a right ideal: it absorbs right multiplication by arbitrary ring elements, and a ∈ J.
    - For any r,s ∈ R, ras = r(as). Since as ∈ J by the previous bullet and J is a left ideal, multiplying on the left by r yields r(as) = ras ∈ J.
    - Therefore, every generator na, ra, as, ras belongs to J. Since J is an additive subgroup, it is closed under finite sums of its own elements, so every finite sum of generators — i.e., every element of S — belongs to J. Thus S ⊆ J.

11. The principal ideal (a) is defined as the intersection of all ideals of R that contain a: (a) = ⋂{J ◁ R | a ∈ J}. Equivalently, (a) is the unique smallest ideal of R containing a, with respect to set inclusion.

12. From step 8, S is an ideal containing a (step 9). From step 10, S is contained in every ideal that contains a. Therefore S is the smallest ideal containing a. By uniqueness of the smallest ideal containing a, S = (a).
∎
original_proof: Show the set S of all such finite sums satisfies the ideal criteria, contains a, and is contained in every ideal containing a; thus S = (a).

name: Purely transcendental extension
statement: A field F is called a purely transcendental extension of a field K if F = K(S), where S ⊂ F and S is algebraically independent over K.
original_proof: N/A

name: Equicardinality of finite transcendence bases
statement: Let F be an extension field of K. If S is a finite transcendence base of F over K, then every transcendence base of F over K has the same number of elements as S.
gapfill_proof:
1. Restate the definitions. A subset A ⊆ F is algebraically independent over K if for every finite subset {a₁, …, a_m} ⊆ A and every nonzero polynomial f ∈ K[X₁, …, X_m], the evaluation f(a₁, …, a_m) is nonzero in F. A transcendence base of F over K is a subset B ⊆ F such that (i) B is algebraically independent over K, and (ii) the field extension F / K(B) is algebraic, where K(B) denotes the subfield of F generated by K ∪ B.

2. Let S = {s₁, …, s_n} be a finite transcendence base of F over K (given by hypothesis). Let T be an arbitrary transcendence base of F over K. The goal is to prove that T is finite and |T| = n.

3. Recall the Tower Theorem for algebraic extensions (Transitivity of Algebraicity): if E/L and L/K are algebraic field extensions, then E/K is algebraic. This theorem will be used repeatedly.

4. State the Exchange Lemma for algebraic independence. Let A ⊆ F be an algebraically independent set over K and let x ∈ F. If x is algebraic over K(A) but x is not algebraic over K, then there exists an element a ∈ A such that A′ = (A \ {a}) ∪ {x} is algebraically independent over K and a is algebraic over K(A′).

5. Prove the Exchange Lemma. Since x is algebraic over K(A), there exists a nonzero polynomial with coefficients in K(A) that vanishes at x. By clearing denominators, obtain finitely many elements a₁, …, a_r ∈ A and a nonzero polynomial F ∈ K[X₁, …, X_r, Y] such that F(a₁, …, a_r, x) = 0 in F. Choose such an F with the minimal possible number r of elements from A that appear (the minimum total degree or number of monomials is not needed; only minimality of the size of the supporting subset of A).

6. Argue that r ≥ 1 in step 5. If r = 0, then F is a nonzero polynomial in K[Y] alone and F(x) = 0, which makes x algebraic over K, contradicting the hypothesis of the Exchange Lemma that x is not algebraic over K. Hence r ≥ 1.

7. Let a = a_r be the (r-th) element of A that appears in F. View F as a polynomial in the variable X_r with coefficients in K[X₁, …, X_{r−1}, Y]: write F = ∑_{j=0}^d G_j(X₁, …, X_{r−1}, Y) · X_r^j with each G_j ∈ K[X₁, …, X_{r−1}, Y] and with G_d not the zero polynomial (otherwise the degree in X_r would be lower).

8. Suppose, for contradiction, that G_d(a₁, …, a_{r−1}, x) = 0. Then the tuple (a₁, …, a_{r−1}, x) satisfies the nonzero polynomial equation G_d = 0. Since G_d involves only r−1 elements from A (namely a₁, …, a_{r−1}) together with x, this would mean that x is algebraic over K(a₁, …, a_{r−1}) = K(A′) where A′ = A \ {a_r} (but we have not yet proven algebraic independence). Because the number of elements from A involved in G_d is at most r−1, the minimality of r (from step 5) would be contradicted unless G_d is identically zero as a polynomial. But G_d was chosen as the nonzero leading coefficient. Hence G_d(a₁, …, a_{r−1}, x) ≠ 0.

9. The nonvanishing G_d(a₁, …, a_{r−1}, x) ≠ 0 means that the relation F(a₁, …, a_r, x) = 0 from step 5 is a nontrivial algebraic equation for a_r over the field K(a₁, …, a_{r−1}, x). Consequently, a = a_r is algebraic over K(A \ {a}, x) = K(A′). This proves the second part of the Exchange Lemma.

10. Prove that A′ = (A \ {a}) ∪ {x} is algebraically independent over K. Suppose to the contrary that A′ is algebraically dependent. Then there exists a finite subset {u₁, …, u_s} ⊆ A \ {a} and a nonzero polynomial H ∈ K[Y₁, …, Y_s, Z] such that H(u₁, …, u_s, x) = 0. Because A itself is algebraically independent, the polynomial H must nontrivially involve the variable Z (corresponding to x); otherwise H(u₁, …, u_s) = 0 would already contradict the algebraic independence of A.

11. The nontrivial appearance of Z in H means that H(u₁, …, u_s, x) = 0 witnesses x as algebraic over K(u₁, …, u_s) ⊆ K(A \ {a}). Thus x is algebraic over K(A \ {a}).

12. Combine steps 9 and 11. Step 9 gives that a is algebraic over K(A \ {a}, x), and step 11 gives that x is algebraic over K(A \ {a}). Applying the Tower Theorem (step 3) to the chain K(A \ {a}) ⊆ K(A \ {a}, x) ⊆ K(A \ {a}, x)(a) = K(A), we deduce that a is algebraic over K(A \ {a}). This contradicts the hypothesis that A is algebraically independent over K (since a ∈ A). Therefore A′ must be algebraically independent over K. The Exchange Lemma is proved.

13. Return to the main theorem. The proof now proceeds by induction to show that |T| ≤ n. Construct a sequence of transcendence bases S₀, S₁, …, S_k (for some k ≤ n), where each S_i has exactly n elements and is obtained from S_{i−1} by exchanging one element of S_{i−1} for a new element from T.

14. Define S₀ = S = {s₁, …, s_n}. By hypothesis, S₀ is a transcendence base of F over K. This is the base case of the induction (i = 0).

15. Induction hypothesis. Assume for some i ≥ 0 that we have constructed a transcendence base S_i = {t₁, …, t_i, s_{i+1}, …, s_n} of F over K, where t₁, …, t_i are i distinct elements of T and s_{i+1}, …, s_n are n−i elements of the original set S (after a suitable permutation of the indices of the original s_j).

16. If the i elements t₁, …, t_i already exhaust T, then |T| = i ≤ n and the desired inequality |T| ≤ n is already established. Otherwise, there exists an element t ∈ T \ {t₁, …, t_i}. (Note: if T is infinite, such a t always exists.)

17. Because S_i is a transcendence base, F is algebraic over K(S_i). In particular, the element t ∈ F is algebraic over K(S_i) = K(t₁, …, t_i, s_{i+1}, …, s_n). This follows from the definition of transcendence base (step 1, condition (ii)).

18. Because T is algebraically independent over K, the element t ∈ T is not algebraic over the subfield K(t₁, …, t_i). For if t were algebraic over K(t₁, …, t_i), then the set {t₁, …, t_i, t} ⊆ T would be algebraically dependent, contradicting the algebraic independence of T (Definition, step 1).

19. Apply the Exchange Lemma (steps 4–12) with A = S_i = {t₁, …, t_i, s_{i+1}, …, s_n} and x = t. The hypotheses are satisfied: A is algebraically independent (S_i is a transcendence base), t is algebraic over K(A) (step 17), and t is not algebraic over K (step 18, noting that if t were algebraic over K, it would certainly be algebraic over K(t₁, …, t_i), which step 18 has already excluded). The lemma yields an element w ∈ S_i such that S_i′ = (S_i \ {w}) ∪ {t} is algebraically independent and w is algebraic over K(S_i′).

20. Prove that the element w provided by the Exchange Lemma cannot be one of the previously exchanged elements t₁, …, t_i. Suppose, for contradiction, that w = t_j for some j ∈ {1, …, i}. Then t_j is algebraic over K(S_i′) = K(t₁, …, t_{j−1}, t_{j+1}, …, t_i, s_{i+1}, …, s_n, t). In particular, t_j is algebraic over a field generated (over K) by the set {t₁, …, t_{j−1}, t_{j+1}, …, t_i, t} together with the s's. Since t_j is algebraic over a field that contains the other t_r's and t, the set {t₁, …, t_i, t} ⊆ T would be algebraically dependent over K (by the Tower Theorem — an algebraic relation among the generators translates to algebraic dependence of the set). More precisely: if t_j satisfies a nonzero polynomial over K(t₁, …, t_{j−1}, t_{j+1}, …, t_i, t, s_{i+1}, …, s_n), then by clearing denominators we obtain an algebraic relation among {t₁, …, t_i, t} ∪ {s_{i+1}, …, s_n}. If the s's do not all cancel trivially, this contradicts the algebraic independence of S_i = {t₁, …, t_i, s_{i+1}, …, s_n}. Since the s's are algebraically independent from {t₁, …, t_i} (they are part of the algebraically independent set S_i), a nontrivial polynomial relation involving both t_j and the s's cannot hold with the s's eliminated unless the relation also involves the s's nontrivially. The only way to avoid contradicting the algebraic independence of S_i is if the relation does not involve any s, i.e., t_j is algebraic over K(t₁, …, t_{j−1}, t_{j+1}, …, t_i, t) alone. But then {t₁, …, t_i, t} is algebraically dependent over K, contradicting the algebraic independence of T. Therefore w ∉ {t₁, …, t_i}.

21. Since w ∈ S_i and w ∉ {t₁, …, t_i}, the element w must be one of s_{i+1}, …, s_n. After renumbering the remaining s-elements, we may assume without loss of generality that w = s_{i+1}. Define t_{i+1} = t (the element chosen from T in step 16) and set S_{i+1} = {t₁, …, t_i, t_{i+1}, s_{i+2}, …, s_n}.

22. Verify that S_{i+1} is a transcendence base of F over K. Algebraic independence of S_{i+1} follows directly from step 19 (the property S_i′ is algebraically independent). For the second property (F is algebraic over K(S_{i+1})): by the induction hypothesis, F is algebraic over K(S_i) = K(t₁, …, t_i, s_{i+1}, …, s_n). The Exchange Lemma gives that s_{i+1} is algebraic over K(S_{i+1}) (this is the second conclusion of the lemma: w is algebraic over K(S_i′)). Hence K(S_i) = K(S_{i+1})(s_{i+1}) is an algebraic extension of K(S_{i+1}). By the Tower Theorem (step 3), the algebraicity of F over K(S_i) and of K(S_i) over K(S_{i+1}) together imply that F is algebraic over K(S_{i+1}). Thus S_{i+1} is a transcendence base, completing the induction step.

23. Termination of the induction. The construction in steps 15–22 either halts because all elements of T have been placed (step 16, giving |T| = i ≤ n) or continues until i reaches n. If the induction reaches i = n, then S_n = {t₁, …, t_n} ⊆ T is a transcendence base of size n. If T contained any additional element t′ ∈ T \ {t₁, …, t_n}, then t′ would be algebraic over K(S_n) = K(t₁, …, t_n) (because S_n is a transcendence base, so F is algebraic over K(S_n)), which would contradict the algebraic independence of T (since t′ together with {t₁, …, t_n} would then be algebraically dependent). Therefore T cannot contain more than n elements. In either case, |T| ≤ n.

24. Finiteness of T. The previous step shows |T| ≤ n, so T is finite. (If T were infinite, one could select n+1 distinct elements from T; these would form an algebraically independent set of size n+1, and repeating the induction argument with this subset in place of T would yield n+1 ≤ n, a contradiction. Hence T cannot be infinite.)

25. Symmetry argument. The entire argument up to step 24 used only that S is a finite transcendence base and T is a transcendence base. The roles of S and T are symmetric: T is now known to be a finite transcendence base (step 24) and S is a transcendence base. Applying the same reasoning with the roles of S and T interchanged yields |S| ≤ |T|.

26. Specifically, let S′ = T and T′ = S. Then S′ is a finite transcendence base of size |T|, and T′ is a transcendence base. By the argument of steps 13–23 applied to (S′, T′) in place of (S, T), we obtain |T′| ≤ |S′|, i.e., |S| ≤ |T|.

27. Combine the two inequalities |T| ≤ |S| (step 23) and |S| ≤ |T| (step 26). By the antisymmetry of the ≤ relation on natural numbers, |S| = |T| = n. Every transcendence base of F over K has exactly n elements. ∎
original_proof: Proof continues beyond the excerpt provided.

name: Middle Linear Map
statement: If A_R and _RB are modules over a ring R, and C is an (additive) abelian group, then a middle linear map from A × B to C is a function f : A × B → C such that for all a, a_i ∈ A, b, b_i ∈ B, and r ∈ R: f(a_1 + a_2, b) = f(a_1, b) + f(a_2, b); f(a, b_1 + b_2) = f(a, b_1) + f(a, b_2); f(ar, b) = f(a, rb).
original_proof: N/A

name: Quotient Monoid/Group by a Congruence
statement: Let R be an equivalence relation on a monoid G such that a1 ~ a2 and b1 ~ b2 imply a1 b1 ~ a2 b2 for all a_i, b_i ∈ G. Then the set G/R of equivalence classes with operation (a)(b) = ab is a monoid. Moreover, if G is a group, G/R is a group; if G is abelian, G/R is abelian.
gapfill_proof:
1. Let G be a monoid with binary operation · and identity element e ∈ G. Let R be an equivalence relation on G satisfying the congruence condition: for all a₁, a₂, b₁, b₂ ∈ G, if a₁ R a₂ and b₁ R b₂, then (a₁·b₁) R (a₂·b₂).

name: Radical of a Primary Submodule is a Primary Ideal

name: Center of Nontrivial Finite p-Groups (Corollary 5.4)
statement: The center C(G) of a nontrivial finite p-group G contains more than one element.
gapfill_proof:
1. Apply the Conjugacy Class Equation to the finite group G. The conjugacy classes partition G. The center C(G) = {z ∈ G | zg = gz for all g ∈ G} consists precisely of the elements whose conjugacy class has size 1. Select representatives x₁, …, xₖ for all conjugacy classes of size strictly greater than 1. The class equation is: |G| = |C(G)| + Σ_{i=1}^{k} |cl(xᵢ)|, where cl(xᵢ) is the conjugacy class of xᵢ.

2. By the Orbit-Stabilizer Theorem applied to the action of G on itself by conjugation (g · x = gxg⁻¹), the orbit of xᵢ is its conjugacy class cl(xᵢ) and its stabilizer is its centralizer C_G(xᵢ) = {g ∈ G | gxᵢ = xᵢg}. Hence |cl(xᵢ)| = [G : C_G(xᵢ)]. Substituting into the class equation: |G| = |C(G)| + Σ_{i=1}^{k} [G : C_G(xᵢ)].

3. For each representative xᵢ chosen outside C(G), the centralizer C_G(xᵢ) is a proper subgroup of G. Proof by contrapositive: if C_G(xᵢ) = G then xᵢ commutes with every element of G, placing xᵢ in C(G), contradicting its selection. Since C_G(xᵢ) is a proper subgroup, Lagrange's Theorem gives [G : C_G(xᵢ)] = |G| / |C_G(xᵢ)| > 1.

4. By hypothesis, G is a nontrivial finite p-group. By definition of a p-group, |G| = pⁿ for a fixed prime p and an integer n ≥ 1 (nontrivial means n ≠ 0).

5. For each i, C_G(xᵢ) is a subgroup of G, so by Lagrange's Theorem its order |C_G(xᵢ)| divides |G| = pⁿ. The index [G : C_G(xᵢ)] = |G| / |C_G(xᵢ)| is therefore an integer divisor of pⁿ, so each [G : C_G(xᵢ)] equals p^{mᵢ} for some integer mᵢ ≥ 0.

6. From Step 3, [G : C_G(xᵢ)] > 1, so mᵢ ≥ 1. Thus each index [G : C_G(xᵢ)] = p^{mᵢ} with mᵢ ≥ 1, and is therefore divisible by p. In symbols: p | [G : C_G(xᵢ)] for every i = 1, …, k.

7. A basic property of divisibility in the integers: if p divides each term of a finite sum, then p divides the sum. Applying this to Step 6: p | Σ_{i=1}^{k} [G : C_G(xᵢ)].

8. Since |G| = pⁿ with n ≥ 1 (from Step 4), p divides |G|.

9. Rearrange the class equation from Step 2 to isolate |C(G)|: |C(G)| = |G| − Σ_{i=1}^{k} [G : C_G(xᵢ)].

10. The right-hand side is an integer linear combination of multiples of p: p divides |G| (Step 8) and p divides the sum Σ [G : C_G(xᵢ)] (Step 7). By the property of divisibility that a linear combination of multiples of an integer is itself a multiple of that integer, p divides their difference: p | |C(G)|.

11. C(G) is a subgroup of G (the identity e ∈ C(G) since e commutes with all elements; if a, b ∈ C(G) then for any g ∈ G, (ab)g = a(bg) = a(gb) = (ag)b = (ga)b = g(ab), so ab ∈ C(G); if a ∈ C(G) then ag = ga implies a⁻¹g = ga⁻¹ by left- and right-multiplying by a⁻¹, so a⁻¹ ∈ C(G)). Thus |C(G)| ≥ 1.

12. From Steps 10 and 11, |C(G)| is a positive integer divisible by p. The smallest positive integer divisible by p is p itself. Therefore |C(G)| ≥ p.

13. Since p is a prime dividing |G| = pⁿ with n ≥ 1, we have p ≥ 2. Hence |C(G)| ≥ p ≥ 2 > 1. The center C(G) therefore contains at least the identity e and at least one non-identity element. This establishes Corollary 5.4: the center of a nontrivial finite p-group contains more than one element. ∎
original_proof: Class equation: |G| = |C(G)| + Σ [G:C_G(x_i)]. Each [G:C_G(x_i)] > 1 divides |G| = p^n, so p divides each and |G|, thus p | |C(G)|. Since |C(G)| ≥ 1, we have |C(G)| ≥ p.

name: Theorem 1.6 (Structure of Subgroups of Finitely Generated Free Abelian Groups)
statement: If F is a free abelian group of finite rank n and G is a nonzero subgroup of F, then there exists a basis {x_1, ..., x_n} of F, an integer r (1 ≤ r ≤ n), and positive integers d_1, ..., d_r such that d_1 | d_2 | ... | d_r and G is free abelian with basis {d_1 x_1, ..., d_r x_r}.
gapfill_proof:
1. Proceed by induction on the rank n of F. Assume F is a free abelian group of rank n and G ≤ F is a nonzero subgroup.

2. [Base case: n = 1] Then F ≅ ℤ. Every subgroup of ℤ is of the form mℤ for a unique nonnegative integer m (Theorem: Classification of Subgroups of ℤ). Since G ≠ {0}, there exists a positive integer d₁ such that G = ⟨d₁ x₁⟩ where {x₁} is any fixed basis of F. Set r = 1. The divisibility condition is vacuous, and the conclusion holds. ∎ (Base case established.)

3. [Induction hypothesis] Assume the theorem holds for all free abelian groups of rank strictly less than n, where n ≥ 2.

4. [Define the set S of first coefficients] Let S be the set of all integers s for which there exists a basis {y₁, …, yₙ} of F and an element g ∈ G such that, when g is expressed in this basis, the coefficient of y₁ equals s. Formally: S = {s ∈ ℤ : ∃ basis {y₁,…,yₙ} of F and g = s y₁ + c₂ y₂ + … + cₙ yₙ ∈ G}.

5. [S contains a positive integer] Since G is nonzero, pick any nonzero g ∈ G. Express g in any fixed basis of F. At least one coordinate of g is nonzero. By permuting the basis vectors so that a nonzero coordinate appears in the first position, and replacing g by −g if the first coordinate is negative, we obtain a positive integer belonging to S. Hence S ∩ ℕ is nonempty.

6. [Choose the minimal positive element d₁] Let d₁ = min(S ∩ ℕ). By step 5, d₁ ≥ 1. Select a basis {y₁, …, yₙ} of F and an element g ∈ G such that g = d₁ y₁ + c₂ y₂ + … + cₙ yₙ, where cᵢ ∈ ℤ.

7. [Claim 1: d₁ divides every coefficient cᵢ] For each i ∈ {2, …, n}, apply the Division Algorithm to write cᵢ = qᵢ d₁ + rᵢ with 0 ≤ rᵢ < d₁.

8. [Basis change] Form a new basis of F by replacing y₁ with z = y₁ + q₂ y₂ + … + qₙ yₙ. Verify that {z, y₂, …, yₙ} is a basis of F: the transition matrix from {y₁, …, yₙ} is unimodular (upper triangular with ones on the diagonal), hence its determinant is 1 and it defines an automorphism of ℤⁿ.

9. [Express g in the new basis] Substitute y₁ = z − q₂ y₂ − … − qₙ yₙ into the expression for g: g = d₁(z − q₂ y₂ − … − qₙ yₙ) + c₂ y₂ + … + cₙ yₙ = d₁ z + (c₂ − d₁ q₂) y₂ + … + (cₙ − d₁ qₙ) yₙ = d₁ z + r₂ y₂ + … + rₙ yₙ.

10. [Minimality forces all remainders to be zero] If any rᵢ > 0, then permute the basis {z, y₂, …, yₙ} so that the basis vector yᵢ (with coefficient rᵢ) occupies the first position. This yields an element of G whose first coordinate is rᵢ, a positive integer strictly smaller than d₁, which belongs to S. This contradicts the definition of d₁ as the minimal positive element of S. Therefore every rᵢ = 0. Consequently cᵢ = qᵢ d₁ for each i, proving that d₁ divides cᵢ.

11. [Obtain a basis element d₁ x₁ ∈ G] Set x₁ = z = y₁ + q₂ y₂ + … + qₙ yₙ. Then by step 9, g = d₁ x₁. The set {x₁, y₂, …, yₙ} is a basis of F (same unimodular change as step 8). Define H = ⟨y₂, …, yₙ⟩, the free abelian subgroup of F generated by the last n−1 basis vectors. Then H is free abelian of rank n−1, and F = ⟨x₁⟩ ⊕ H (internal direct sum), because {x₁, y₂, …, yₙ} is a basis.

12. [Claim 2: G = ⟨d₁ x₁⟩ ⊕ (G ∩ H)] Take any g ∈ G. Express g in the basis {x₁, y₂, …, yₙ}: g = a x₁ + h where a ∈ ℤ and h ∈ H.

13. [Division algorithm on the x₁-coordinate] Apply the Division Algorithm to write a = q d₁ + r with 0 ≤ r < d₁. Then g − q(d₁ x₁) = r x₁ + h. Since g ∈ G and d₁ x₁ ∈ G (step 11), the difference r x₁ + h belongs to G.

14. [Minimality forces r = 0] The element r x₁ + h has first coordinate r in the basis {x₁, y₂, …, yₙ}. By definition of S, r ∈ S ∪ {0}. Since 0 ≤ r < d₁ and d₁ is the minimum positive element of S, we must have r = 0. Thus a = q d₁, and h = g − q d₁ x₁ ∈ G because both g and d₁ x₁ are elements of the subgroup G.

15. [Inclusion G ⊆ ⟨d₁ x₁⟩ + (G ∩ H)] From step 14, h ∈ G (by closure of G) and h ∈ H (by definition), so h ∈ G ∩ H. Therefore g = q d₁ x₁ + h ∈ ⟨d₁ x₁⟩ + (G ∩ H). This holds for all g ∈ G, so G ⊆ ⟨d₁ x₁⟩ + (G ∩ H).

16. [Reverse inclusion] The group ⟨d₁ x₁⟩ is contained in G because d₁ x₁ ∈ G (step 11). The subgroup G ∩ H is contained in G by definition. Since G is closed under addition, every sum of an element from ⟨d₁ x₁⟩ and an element from G ∩ H lies in G. Hence ⟨d₁ x₁⟩ + (G ∩ H) ⊆ G.

17. [Directness of the sum] Suppose w ∈ ⟨d₁ x₁⟩ ∩ (G ∩ H). Write w = k d₁ x₁ for some k ∈ ℤ, and also w ∈ H. Since ⟨x₁⟩ ∩ H = {0} (the sum F = ⟨x₁⟩ ⊕ H is direct), the element k d₁ x₁ belongs to H only if k d₁ = 0. Since d₁ > 0, we deduce k = 0, so w = 0. Therefore ⟨d₁ x₁⟩ ∩ (G ∩ H) = {0}. Together with steps 15–16, this establishes G = ⟨d₁ x₁⟩ ⊕ (G ∩ H).

18. [Apply induction hypothesis to G ∩ H] The subgroup G ∩ H is a subgroup of the free abelian group H of rank n−1. Apply the induction hypothesis (step 3) to G ∩ H ⊆ H. There exists a basis {x₂, …, xₙ} of H, an integer r' with 1 ≤ r' ≤ n−1, and positive integers d₂, …, d_{r'+1} such that d₂ | d₃ | … | d_{r'+1} and G ∩ H = ⟨d₂ x₂, …, d_{r'+1} x_{r'+1}⟩. Re-index by setting r = r' + 1, so G ∩ H = ⟨d₂ x₂, …, dᵣ xᵣ⟩ with d₂ | d₃ | … | dᵣ and 2 ≤ r ≤ n.

19. [Claim 3: d₁ divides d₂] The element d₂ x₂ belongs to G ∩ H ⊆ G. Consider the basis of F obtained by swapping x₁ and x₂: {x₂, x₁, x₃, …, xₙ}. In this basis, d₂ x₂ = d₂ · x₂ + 0 · x₁ + … has first coordinate d₂. Hence d₂ ∈ S. By definition of d₁ as the minimal positive element of S, we conclude d₁ ≤ d₂.

20. [Use Claim 1 to establish divisibility] Consider the sum d₁ x₁ + d₂ x₂. Both d₁ x₁ and d₂ x₂ belong to G (step 11 and step 18), so their sum lies in G. Express this sum in the basis {x₁, x₂, …, xₙ}: d₁ x₁ + d₂ x₂ = d₁ x₁ + d₂ x₂ + 0 · x₃ + … + 0 · xₙ. The coefficient of x₁ is d₁, which is the minimal positive element of S. Apply Claim 1 (steps 7–10) to this expression: whenever an element of G has first coordinate equal to d₁ in some basis, d₁ must divide all remaining coefficients. Hence d₁ divides the coefficient of x₂, which is d₂. Therefore d₁ | d₂.

21. [Combine with the chain of divisibilities from the induction hypothesis] From step 18 we have d₂ | d₃ | … | dᵣ, and from step 20 we have d₁ | d₂. Transitivity of divisibility yields d₁ | d₂ | d₃ | … | dᵣ.

22. [Assemble the basis of F] The set {x₁, x₂, …, xₙ} is a basis of F because: {x₁, y₂, …, yₙ} is a basis (step 11), {x₂, …, xₙ} is a basis of H = ⟨y₂, …, yₙ⟩ (step 18), and the two together span F with the correct rank n. Every element of F can be written uniquely as a linear combination of x₁, …, xₙ.

23. [Assemble the basis of G] Since G = ⟨d₁ x₁⟩ ⊕ (G ∩ H) (steps 15–17) and G ∩ H = ⟨d₂ x₂, …, dᵣ xᵣ⟩ (step 18), we have G = ⟨d₁ x₁⟩ ⊕ ⟨d₂ x₂⟩ ⊕ … ⊕ ⟨dᵣ xᵣ⟩. The set {d₁ x₁, d₂ x₂, …, dᵣ xᵣ} is linearly independent over ℤ (they are nonzero multiples of distinct basis vectors of a free abelian group) and generates G, hence it is a basis of G.

24. [Verify parameters] The integer r satisfies 1 ≤ r ≤ n (from the induction: r' ≤ n−1, so r = r'+1 ≤ n, and G nonzero ensures r ≥ 1). Each dᵢ is a positive integer, and the divisibility chain d₁ | d₂ | … | dᵣ is established in step 21. The basis {x₁, …, xₙ} of F and the basis {d₁ x₁, …, dᵣ xᵣ} of G satisfy all required conditions.

∎
original_proof: By induction on n. If n=1, G = ⟨d_1 x_1⟩. For n>1, let S be the set of integers s such that some basis {y_i} yields an element in G with coefficient s for y_1. Let d_1 be the least positive element in S. Using Lemma 1.5, choose basis {x_1, y_2, ..., y_n} with d_1 x_1 ∈ G, set H = ⟨y_2, ..., y_n⟩, so F = ⟨x_1⟩ ⊕ H. Prove G = ⟨d_1 x_1⟩ ⊕ (G ∩ H). Apply the induction hypothesis to G ∩ H in H, obtaining a basis {x_2, ..., x_n} of H and d_2 | ... | d_r with G ∩ H = ⟨d_2 x_2, ..., d_r x_r⟩. Minimality of d_1 forces d_1 | d_2. Then {x_1, ..., x_n} and {d_1 x_1, ..., d_r x_r} are the required bases.

name: Inner Automorphism and Center Theorem
statement: (i) For each g ∈ G, conjugation by g induces an automorphism of G. (ii) There is a homomorphism G → Aut G whose kernel is C(G) = {g ∈ G | gx = xg for all x ∈ G}.
gapfill_proof:
1. For a fixed element g ∈ G, define the map T_g: G → G by the rule T_g(x) = gxg⁻¹ for all x ∈ G. This is the conjugation-by-g map. [Definition of conjugation action]
2. Verify that T_g is a group homomorphism. For any x, y ∈ G, compute T_g(xy) = g(xy)g⁻¹. By the associativity axiom of the group G [Group Axiom: (ab)c = a(bc)], rewrite g(xy)g⁻¹ = (gx)(yg⁻¹). Insert the identity g⁻¹g = e [Group Axiom: existence of inverses] to obtain gx(g⁻¹g)yg⁻¹. Apply associativity again: (gxg⁻¹)(gyg⁻¹). This equals T_g(x) · T_g(y). Hence T_g(xy) = T_g(x)T_g(y), so T_g respects the group operation. [Definition of group homomorphism]
3. Verify that T_g is injective. Suppose T_g(x) = T_g(y) for some x, y ∈ G. Then gxg⁻¹ = gyg⁻¹. Multiply on the left by g⁻¹ and on the right by g. By the left and right cancellation laws, which follow from the group axioms, this yields x = y. Therefore T_g is injective. [Definition of injective function; Group cancellation lemma]
4. Verify that T_g is surjective. Let y ∈ G be an arbitrary element. Set x = g⁻¹yg, which is an element of G by closure [Group Axiom: closure under multiplication]. Then T_g(x) = g(g⁻¹yg)g⁻¹ = (gg⁻¹)y(gg⁻¹) = e y e = y, using associativity and the definition of inverses and identity [Group Axioms: associativity, inverses, identity]. Thus every y ∈ G has a preimage x, so T_g is surjective. [Definition of surjective function]
5. Equivalently, the bijectivity of T_g follows from Theorem 4.5. Theorem 4.5 states that for any group G, the left multiplication map L_g: G → G defined by L_g(x) = gx is a bijection. Likewise, right multiplication R_{g⁻¹}: G → G defined by R_{g⁻¹}(x) = xg⁻¹ is a bijection. The conjugation map satisfies T_g = L_g ∘ R_{g⁻¹}, since (L_g ∘ R_{g⁻¹})(x) = L_g(xg⁻¹) = gxg⁻¹ = T_g(x). The composition of two bijections is a bijection [Composition-of-bijections lemma]. Therefore T_g is a bijection.
6. A function f: G → G that is both a group homomorphism and a bijection is called an automorphism of G. Steps 2 and 3–5 establish that T_g satisfies both conditions. Hence T_g ∈ Aut(G). This proves claim (i): for each g ∈ G, conjugation by g induces an automorphism of G. [Definition of group automorphism]
7. Now define a map φ: G → Aut(G) by φ(g) = T_g, where T_g is the inner automorphism constructed in claim (i). [Definition of the map φ]
8. By claim (i), T_g ∈ Aut(G) for every g ∈ G, so the rule φ(g) = T_g is well-defined and maps G into Aut(G). [Well-definedness from claim (i)]
9. Show that φ is a group homomorphism. Take any g, h ∈ G. For every x ∈ G, compute φ(gh)(x) = T_{gh}(x) = (gh)x(gh)⁻¹. By the identity (gh)⁻¹ = h⁻¹g⁻¹ [Group Axiom: inverse of a product], this equals gh x h⁻¹g⁻¹. By associativity [Group Axiom: (ab)c = a(bc)], group terms as g (h x h⁻¹) g⁻¹. Recognize h x h⁻¹ = T_h(x), so the expression becomes g · T_h(x) · g⁻¹ = T_g(T_h(x)) = (T_g ∘ T_h)(x). Thus T_{gh} = T_g ∘ T_h, which means φ(gh) = φ(g) ∘ φ(h) for all g, h ∈ G. [Definition of group homomorphism; Definition of function composition in Aut(G)]
10. Compute the kernel of φ. By definition, ker(φ) = {g ∈ G | φ(g) = id_G}, where id_G is the identity automorphism of G (the identity function on G). [Definition of kernel of a group homomorphism]
11. The condition φ(g) = id_G means T_g(x) = x for every x ∈ G. [Definition of equality of functions]
12. Substitute the definition of T_g: the equation T_g(x) = x becomes gxg⁻¹ = x for every x ∈ G. [Definition of T_g from step 1]
13. Multiply both sides of gxg⁻¹ = x on the right by g. By the group operation and the definition of the inverse, x(g⁻¹g) = xg, so the left side becomes gx and the right side becomes xg. Thus gx = xg for every x ∈ G. [Group Axioms: associativity and inverses]
14. By definition, the center C(G) of a group G is the set {g ∈ G | gx = xg for all x ∈ G}. The condition derived in step 13 exactly defines membership in C(G). Therefore ker(φ) = C(G). [Definition of the center of a group]
15. In summary, φ: G → Aut(G) is a group homomorphism (established in steps 7–9) whose kernel is C(G) (established in steps 10–14). This proves claim (ii): there exists a homomorphism G → Aut(G) whose kernel is the center of G. ∎
original_proof: (i) Act on G by conjugation. The map T_g(x) = gxg⁻¹ is a bijection by Theorem 4.5 and a homomorphism since g(xy)g⁻¹ = (gxg⁻¹)(gyg⁻¹). Thus T_g ∈ Aut G. (ii) The image of the homomorphism G → A(G) lies in Aut G. Its kernel is {g | T_g = id} = {g | gxg⁻¹ = x ∀x} = C(G).

name: Definition of Contraction and Lies Over
statement: If S is an extension ring of R and I is an ideal of S, then I ∩ R is an ideal of R called the contraction of I to R, and I is said to lie over J = I ∩ R. If Q is a prime ideal in S, then Q ∩ R is a prime ideal of R.
original_proof: N/A

name: Characterization of Injective Modules over Rings with Identity (Baer's Criterion)
statement: Let R be a ring with identity. A unitary R-module J is injective if and only if for every left ideal L of R, any R-module homomorphism f: L -> J may be extended to an R-module homomorphism R -> J.
gapfill_proof:
1. Recall the definition of an injective module (Definition of Injective Module): an R-module J is injective if and only if for every R-module monomorphism g: A → B and every R-module homomorphism f: A → J, there exists an R-module homomorphism f': B → J such that f' ∘ g = f.

2. (⇒ Forward direction) Assume J is an injective R-module. Let L be any left ideal of R. Let f: L → J be any R-module homomorphism. The inclusion map ι: L → R (sending each element of L to itself in R) is an R-module monomorphism because L is an R-submodule of R. Apply the definition of injective module (step 1) with A = L, B = R, g = ι, and f = f. This yields an R-module homomorphism f': R → J such that f' ∘ ι = f. Thus f' restricts to f on L, so every homomorphism from a left ideal L to J extends to a homomorphism from R to J.

3. (⇐ Reverse direction) Assume the hypothesis: for every left ideal L of R, every R-module homomorphism f: L → J extends to an R-module homomorphism R → J. We must prove J is injective.

4. Let g: A → B be an arbitrary R-module monomorphism, and let f: A → J be an arbitrary R-module homomorphism. We must construct an extension f': B → J with f' ∘ g = f.

5. Since g is a monomorphism, the map g: A → Im g is an R-module isomorphism. Define ψ: Im g → J by ψ = f ∘ g^{-1}, where g^{-1}: Im g → A is the inverse of the isomorphism A ≅ Im g. Then ψ is an R-module homomorphism and ψ(g(a)) = f(a) for all a ∈ A.

6. Define the set S = { (C, h) | Im g ⊆ C ⊆ B is an R-submodule of B, and h: C → J is an R-module homomorphism such that h|_{Im g} = ψ }. Here h|_{Im g} denotes the restriction of h to Im g. So each element of S is a pair consisting of an intermediate submodule containing Im g and a homomorphism extending ψ.

7. The set S is nonempty: the pair (Im g, ψ) belongs to S because Im g ⊆ Im g ⊆ B (trivially) and ψ|_{Im g} = ψ.

8. Partially order S by the relation ≤ defined as: (C₁, h₁) ≤ (C₂, h₂) if and only if C₁ ⊆ C₂ (as submodules of B) and h₂|_{C₁} = h₁ (i.e., h₂ extends h₁). This relation is reflexive (C ⊆ C, h|_C = h), antisymmetric (if C₁ ⊆ C₂ and C₂ ⊆ C₁ then C₁ = C₂, and the homomorphism condition forces h₁ = h₂), and transitive (if C₁ ⊆ C₂ ⊆ C₃ and h₃ extends h₂ which extends h₁, then h₃ extends h₁).

9. Let T = { (C_i, h_i) }_{i ∈ I} be any chain in S (a totally ordered subset under ≤). Define C_∞ = ⋃_{i ∈ I} C_i. Since the C_i are nested by inclusion (the chain is totally ordered), C_∞ is an R-submodule of B. Define h_∞: C_∞ → J as follows: for any x ∈ C_∞, there exists some i ∈ I with x ∈ C_i; set h_∞(x) = h_i(x). This definition is independent of the choice of i because if x ∈ C_i ∩ C_j, then either C_i ⊆ C_j or C_j ⊆ C_i (total order); in either case h_i(x) = h_j(x) since one extends the other. The map h_∞ is an R-module homomorphism: for x, y ∈ C_∞ and r ∈ R, pick i large enough so that x, y, rx + y all lie in C_i; then h_∞(rx + y) = h_i(rx + y) = r·h_i(x) + h_i(y) = r·h_∞(x) + h_∞(y). Moreover, h_∞|_{Im g} = ψ because each h_i restricts to ψ on Im g. Thus (C_∞, h_∞) ∈ S, and (C_i, h_i) ≤ (C_∞, h_∞) for every i ∈ I. So every chain in S has an upper bound in S.

10. Apply Zorn's Lemma (Axiom of Choice / Zorn's Lemma): a partially ordered set in which every chain has an upper bound contains at least one maximal element. Since S satisfies the hypothesis of Zorn's Lemma by step 9, S possesses a maximal element. Denote it by (H, h_max), where Im g ⊆ H ⊆ B and h_max: H → J is an R-module homomorphism extending ψ.

11. We now prove that H = B. Suppose, for contradiction, that H ≠ B. Then there exists an element b ∈ B \ H.

12. Define the subset L = { r ∈ R | rb ∈ H } of R. Since 0 · b = 0 ∈ H, we have 0 ∈ L, so L is nonempty. For r₁, r₂ ∈ L, we have (r₁ - r₂)b = r₁b - r₂b ∈ H because r₁b ∈ H, r₂b ∈ H, and H is an R-submodule (closed under subtraction). For any s ∈ R and r ∈ L, we have (sr)b = s(rb) ∈ sH ⊆ H because H is an R-submodule. Thus L is a left ideal of R (Definition of Left Ideal).

13. Define the map φ: L → J by φ(r) = h_max(rb) for each r ∈ L. This is well-defined because rb ∈ H by definition of L, and h_max is defined on H. Verify φ is an R-module homomorphism: for r₁, r₂ ∈ L, φ(r₁ + r₂) = h_max((r₁ + r₂)b) = h_max(r₁b + r₂b) = h_max(r₁b) + h_max(r₂b) = φ(r₁) + φ(r₂); and for s ∈ R, r ∈ L, φ(sr) = h_max((sr)b) = h_max(s(rb)) = s·h_max(rb) = s·φ(r) because h_max is an R-module homomorphism.

14. Apply the hypothesis of the theorem (the left-ideal extension property) to the left ideal L and the homomorphism φ: L → J. This yields an R-module homomorphism Φ: R → J such that Φ|_L = φ; that is, Φ(r) = φ(r) = h_max(rb) for all r ∈ L.

15. Define the element c ∈ J by c = Φ(1_R), where 1_R is the multiplicative identity of the ring R (the hypothesis includes that R is a ring with identity).

16. Define the subset H' = H + Rb = { a + rb | a ∈ H, r ∈ R } of B. Since H is an R-submodule, H' is the R-submodule of B generated by H and the element b (Definition of Submodule Generated by a Set). Clearly H ⊆ H' and b = 0 + 1_R·b ∈ H', so H is a proper submodule of H'.

17. Define the map h': H' → J by the formula h'(a + rb) = h_max(a) + r·c, for a ∈ H and r ∈ R. We must verify that h' is well-defined (independent of the representation of an element as a + rb). Suppose a₁ + r₁b = a₂ + r₂b in H' with a₁, a₂ ∈ H and r₁, r₂ ∈ R. Then a₁ - a₂ = (r₂ - r₁)b. Since a₁ - a₂ ∈ H, we obtain (r₂ - r₁)b ∈ H, which means r₂ - r₁ ∈ L by definition of L. Then h_max(a₁ - a₂) = h_max((r₂ - r₁)b). The left side equals h_max(a₁) - h_max(a₂) since h_max is a homomorphism. The right side equals φ(r₂ - r₁) by definition of φ, and Φ(r₂ - r₁) = φ(r₂ - r₁) by step 14. Therefore h_max(a₁) - h_max(a₂) = Φ(r₂ - r₁) = Φ(r₂) - Φ(r₁) = r₂·c - r₁·c. Rearranging: h_max(a₁) + r₁·c = h_max(a₂) + r₂·c. Thus h'(a₁ + r₁b) = h'(a₂ + r₂b), proving h' is well-defined.

18. Verify that h' is an R-module homomorphism. For a₁ + r₁b, a₂ + r₂b ∈ H' and s ∈ R: h'((a₁ + r₁b) + (a₂ + r₂b)) = h'((a₁ + a₂) + (r₁ + r₂)b) = h_max(a₁ + a₂) + (r₁ + r₂)·c = h_max(a₁) + h_max(a₂) + r₁·c + r₂·c = (h_max(a₁) + r₁·c) + (h_max(a₂) + r₂·c) = h'(a₁ + r₁b) + h'(a₂ + r₂b). And h'(s(a₁ + r₁b)) = h'(sa₁ + s(r₁b)) = h'(sa₁ + (sr₁)b) = h_max(sa₁) + (sr₁)·c = s·h_max(a₁) + s·(r₁·c) = s·(h_max(a₁) + r₁·c) = s·h'(a₁ + r₁b).

19. Verify that h' extends h_max: for any a ∈ H, write a = a + 0·b ∈ H'. Then h'(a) = h'(a + 0·b) = h_max(a) + 0·c = h_max(a). Thus h'|_H = h_max.

20. Verify that h' extends ψ: for any x ∈ Im g, we have x ∈ H (since Im g ⊆ H), so h'(x) = h_max(x) = ψ(x) because h_max extends ψ. Thus h'|_{Im g} = ψ.

21. Therefore the pair (H', h') belongs to S. Moreover, (H, h_max) ≤ (H', h') in the partial order: H ⊆ H' (by construction) and h'|_{H} = h_max (by step 19). Since b ∈ H' \ H, the inclusion H ⊆ H' is strict, so (H, h_max) < (H', h'). This contradicts the fact that (H, h_max) is a maximal element of S (step 10).

22. The contradiction obtained in step 21 forces us to reject the assumption H ≠ B. Therefore H = B.

23. Since H = B, the maximal element (H, h_max) is actually a homomorphism h_max: B → J defined on all of B such that h_max|_{Im g} = ψ. Define f': B → J by f' = h_max. For any a ∈ A, f'(g(a)) = h_max(g(a)) = ψ(g(a)) = (f ∘ g^{-1})(g(a)) = f(a). Thus f' ∘ g = f, and f' is the required extension of f.

24. We have shown that for an arbitrary monomorphism g: A → B and an arbitrary homomorphism f: A → J, there exists an extension f': B → J with f' ∘ g = f. By the definition of injective module (step 1), J is injective.

25. Combining the forward direction (step 2) and the reverse direction (steps 3–24), we conclude: an R-module J over a ring R with identity is injective if and only if for every left ideal L of R, every R-module homomorphism f: L → J extends to an R-module homomorphism R → J. ∎
original_proof: If J is injective, the extension property follows directly. Conversely, assume extension property for left ideals. Given a monomorphism g: A -> B and f: A -> J, let S be the set of partial extensions h: C -> J with Im g ⊆ C ⊆ B. Partially order S by extension. S is nonempty since f∘g^{-1}: Im g -> J works. By Zorn's Lemma, a maximal element h: H -> J exists. If H ≠ B, pick b∈B\H, set L = { r∈R | rb∈H } a left ideal. Map r ↦ h(rb) extends to k: R->J by hypothesis. Let c = k(1_R). Define extension on H+Rb by a+rb ↦ h(a)+rc, contradicting maximality. Hence H=B.

name: Characterization of Invertible Matrices as Matrices of Isomorphisms
statement: Let R be a ring with identity and E, F free left R-modules with ordered bases V, V′ respectively such that |V| = n = |V′|. For A ∈ Mat_n R, A is invertible if and only if A is the matrix of an isomorphism f: E → F relative to V and V′. In this case A^{-1} is the matrix of f^{-1} relative to V′ and V.
gapfill_proof:
Let R be a ring with identity, and let E, F be free left R-modules with ordered bases V = (v_1, …, v_n) and V′ = (v_1′, …, v_n′) respectively. Denote by Φ: Hom_R(E, F) → Mat_n(R) the map sending each R-linear map to its matrix relative to V and V′, and by Ψ: Hom_R(F, E) → Mat_n(R) the map sending each R-linear map to its matrix relative to V′ and V.

1. (Setup of Φ and Ψ.) Since V and V′ are ordered bases of size n, the maps Φ and Ψ are R-linear isomorphisms (a standard theorem on matrix representation: choice of bases establishes a bijective correspondence between linear maps and matrices). In particular, Φ is injective: if Φ(h_1) = Φ(h_2) then h_1 = h_2.

2. (Matrix of composition.) For any h: E → F and k: F → E, the matrix of the composition k ∘ h relative to V and V satisfies Ψ(k ∘ h) = Ψ(k) · Φ(h). This is the theorem that matrix multiplication corresponds to composition of linear maps, applied to the specified bases.

3. (Matrix of identity.) The identity matrix I_n ∈ Mat_n(R) is the matrix of the identity map id_E relative to V and V (by definition of the identity matrix: the (i,j)-entry is 1_R if i = j and 0_R otherwise, which matches the coordinate expression of id_E). Likewise, I_n is the matrix of id_F relative to V′ and V′.

--- Direction 1: If A is invertible, then A is the matrix of an isomorphism ---

4. (Inverse matrix exists.) By the definition of invertibility for matrices over a ring, there exists B ∈ Mat_n(R) such that AB = I_n and BA = I_n.

5. (Lift A to a linear map.) Since Φ is surjective, there exists f ∈ Hom_R(E, F) such that Φ(f) = A. That is, A is the matrix of f relative to V and V′.

6. (Lift B to a linear map.) Since Ψ is surjective, there exists g ∈ Hom_R(F, E) such that Ψ(g) = B. That is, B is the matrix of g relative to V′ and V.

7. (Compute Φ(f ∘ g).) Apply the composition theorem (step 2) to the pair (g: E → F and f: F → E — wait, careful with domains): we have f: E → F and g: F → E. Then f ∘ g: F → F. Its matrix relative to V′ and V′ is Φ(f ∘ g) = Φ(f) · Ψ(g) = A · B. By step 4, AB = I_n, so Φ(f ∘ g) = I_n.

8. (Correction of the composition order.) Re-evaluating: Φ maps maps E → F to matrices relative to V, V′. Ψ maps maps F → E to matrices relative to V′, V. For h: E → F, the matrix of h relative to V, V′ is Φ(h). For k: F → E, the matrix of k relative to V′, V is Ψ(k). The composition f ∘ g: F → F has domain F and codomain F; its matrix relative to V′, V′ is denoted M_{V′}^{V′}(f ∘ g). The theorem of matrix of composition, applied with first factor having domain F and codomain E and second factor having domain E and codomain F, gives M_{V′}^{V′}(f ∘ g) = M_{V}^{V′}(f) · M_{V′}^{V}(g) = Φ(f) · Ψ(g) = A · B = I_n.

9. (Deduce f ∘ g = id_F.) The identity map id_F has matrix I_n relative to V′ and V′ (step 3). Thus Φ_{V′,V′}(f ∘ g) = Φ_{V′,V′}(id_F). The matrix representation map relative to a fixed choice of bases is injective (step 1), so f ∘ g = id_F.

10. (Compute the other composition.) Consider g ∘ f: E → E. Its matrix relative to V and V is Ψ(g) · Φ(f) = B · A = I_n (using the appropriate version of the composition theorem). The identity map id_E also has matrix I_n relative to V and V. By injectivity of the matrix representation map, g ∘ f = id_E.

11. (Conclude f is an isomorphism.) Since g ∘ f = id_E and f ∘ g = id_F, g is a two-sided inverse of f. By the definition of an isomorphism in the category of left R-modules, f: E → F is an isomorphism and g = f^{-1}.

12. (Matrix of the inverse.) Because g = f^{-1} and Ψ(g) = B, we have Ψ(f^{-1}) = B = A^{-1}. Thus A^{-1} is the matrix of f^{-1} relative to V′ and V.

--- Direction 2: If A is the matrix of an isomorphism, then A is invertible ---

13. (Assume A comes from an isomorphism.) Suppose A = Φ(f) for some isomorphism f: E → F. By definition of isomorphism, there exists f^{-1}: F → E such that f ∘ f^{-1} = id_F and f^{-1} ∘ f = id_E.

14. (Let B be the matrix of f^{-1}.) Define B = Ψ(f^{-1}), the matrix of f^{-1} relative to V′ and V.

15. (Compute AB.) Using the composition theorem (step 2), A · B = Φ(f) · Ψ(f^{-1}) = M_{V′}^{V′}(f ∘ f^{-1}) = M_{V′}^{V′}(id_F). By step 3, M_{V′}^{V′}(id_F) = I_n. Hence AB = I_n.

16. (Compute BA.) Using the composition theorem with reversed order, B · A = Ψ(f^{-1}) · Φ(f) = M_{V}^{V}(f^{-1} ∘ f) = M_{V}^{V}(id_E) = I_n. Hence BA = I_n.

17. (Conclude A is invertible.) Since AB = I_n and BA = I_n, the matrix A satisfies the definition of an invertible matrix over the ring R, and A^{-1} = B. The matrix A^{-1} is the matrix of f^{-1} relative to V′ and V.

18. (Conclusion.) For any A ∈ Mat_n(R), A is invertible if and only if A is the matrix of an isomorphism f: E → F relative to V and V′, and in this case A^{-1} is the matrix of f^{-1} relative to V′ and V. ∎
original_proof: A homomorphism f is an isomorphism iff it has a two-sided inverse f^{-1}. The matrix of the composition equals the product of matrices; using I_n as matrix of identity maps, A invertible corresponds to existence of inverse map.

name: Natural Anti-isomorphism to Opposite Ring
statement: The identity map on the underlying set, r ↦ r, is an anti-isomorphism from a ring R to its opposite ring R^op.
gapfill_proof:
1. Let R be a ring with addition + and multiplication · . By definition of the opposite ring R^op, its underlying set is identical to that of R, its addition coincides with +, and its multiplication ∘ is defined by a ∘ b = b · a for all a, b ∈ R. [Definition of opposite ring]
2. Define the map θ: R → R^op by θ(r) = r for every r ∈ R. Since the domain and codomain have the same underlying set, θ is the identity function on that set. [Definition of the identity map]
3. Every identity function on a set is bijective. (For any set X, id_X is injective because x ≠ y implies id(x) ≠ id(y), and surjective because every x ∈ X has preimage x.) Therefore θ is bijective. [Set-theoretic property of identity functions]
4. Take arbitrary a, b ∈ R. Compute θ(a + b) = a + b by applying the definition of θ. [Apply definition of θ]
5. Compute θ(a) + θ(b) = a + b, since θ(a) = a and θ(b) = b, and addition in R^op is the same binary operation as addition in R. [Apply definition of θ; definition of opposite ring: addition in R^op equals addition in R]
6. From steps 4 and 5, θ(a + b) = a + b = θ(a) + θ(b). Thus θ preserves addition: θ(a + b) = θ(a) + θ(b) for all a, b ∈ R. [Transitivity of equality; this is the additive homomorphism condition]
7. Take arbitrary a, b ∈ R. Compute θ(a · b) = a · b by applying the definition of θ to the product a · b. [Apply definition of θ]
8. Compute θ(b) ∘ θ(a). Since θ(b) = b and θ(a) = a, we have θ(b) ∘ θ(a) = b ∘ a. By the definition of the opposite ring multiplication, b ∘ a = a · b. [Apply definition of θ twice; then apply definition of ∘ in R^op]
9. From steps 7 and 8, θ(a · b) = a · b = θ(b) ∘ θ(a). Thus θ satisfies θ(a · b) = θ(b) ∘ θ(a) for all a, b ∈ R. [Transitivity of equality]
10. A map φ: A → B between rings is called an anti-homomorphism if φ(a + b) = φ(a) + φ(b) and φ(a · b) = φ(b) · φ(a) for all a, b ∈ A. When the codomain is an opposite ring B^op whose multiplication is ∘, the second condition reads φ(a · b) = φ(b) ∘ φ(a). [Definition of ring anti-homomorphism]
11. Step 6 establishes that θ satisfies the additive condition. Step 9 establishes that θ satisfies the multiplicative anti-homomorphism condition with respect to ∘ in R^op. Therefore θ is an anti-homomorphism from R to R^op. [Combine step 6 and step 9 with the definition from step 10]
12. A bijective anti-homomorphism between rings is called an anti-isomorphism. [Definition of ring anti-isomorphism]
13. Step 3 established that θ is bijective. Step 11 established that θ is an anti-homomorphism. Therefore θ is an anti-isomorphism. [Combine step 3 and step 11 with the definition from step 12]
14. Conclusion: The identity map on the underlying set, r ↦ r, is an anti-isomorphism from a ring R to its opposite ring R^op. ∎
original_proof: The map is bijective and additive. For multiplication: (ab) ↦ ab in R, while a ∘ b = ba, so θ(ab) = ab = b ∘ a = θ(b) ∘ θ(a), satisfying the anti-homomorphism condition.

name: Greatest common divisor in a unique factorization domain
statement: Let a_1, …, a_n be nonzero elements of a unique factorization domain. Write each a_i = u_i p_1^{m_{1i}} p_2^{m_{2i}} ⋯ p_t^{m_{ti}} with a unit u_i and distinct prime elements p_j. Then c = p_1^{k_1} p_2^{k_2} ⋯ p_t^{k_t}, where k_j = min_i m_{ji}, is a greatest common divisor of a_1, …, a_n.
original_proof: The element c divides each a_i because the exponent of each prime in c is the minimum of the exponents in the a_i. Conversely, any common divisor must have each prime exponent at most the minimum, so it divides c.

name: Primary ideal definition
statement: An ideal P in a ring R is called primary if whenever ab ∈ P and a ∉ P, there exists a positive integer n such that b^n ∈ P.

name: Dedekind Domain Definition
statement: A Dedekind domain is an integral domain R in which every ideal (≠ R) is the product of a finite number of prime ideals.
original_proof: None

name: Intersection of Integrally Closed Subrings
statement: Let T be a commutative ring with identity and {S_i}, {R_i} families of subrings such that T is an extension ring of S_i and S_i is an extension ring of R_i for every i. If each R_i is integrally closed in S_i, then ∩ R_i is integrally closed in ∩ S_i.
gapfill_proof:
1. By the definition of integral dependence (Atiyah-Macdonald, p. 59, Definition): an element x of a ring extension S ⊇ R is integral over R if there exists a monic polynomial f(X) = X^n + a_{n-1}X^{n-1} + ... + a_1X + a_0 with all coefficients a_j ∈ R such that f(x) = 0. Assume x ∈ ∩ S_i is integral over ∩ R_i. Then such a monic polynomial f(X) exists with coefficients a_j ∈ ∩ R_i.

2. By the definition of set intersection (Zermelo-Fraenkel axiom of specification / Axiom of Intersection): for any index i, ∩ R_i ⊆ R_i. Hence each coefficient a_j, belonging to ∩ R_i, also belongs to R_i for every i. Thus the polynomial f(X) has coefficients in R_i for every index i.

3. Apply the same definition of integral dependence (Atiyah-Macdonald, p. 59) to the ring pair (R_i, S_i): the element x satisfies f(x) = 0 where f(X) is a monic polynomial with coefficients in R_i (established in Step 2). Therefore x is integral over R_i for each i.

4. Since x ∈ ∩ S_i, by the definition of set intersection, x ∈ S_i for every i. Combined with Step 3, for each i we have: x ∈ S_i and x is integral over R_i.

5. The hypothesis states that each R_i is integrally closed in S_i. By the definition of "integrally closed" (Atiyah-Macdonald, p. 60, or equivalently Matsumura, Commutative Algebra, Definition 9.2): a subring A of B is integrally closed in B if every element of B that is integral over A belongs to A. Applying this definition to the pair (R_i, S_i): if y ∈ S_i is integral over R_i, then y ∈ R_i.

6. For each fixed index i, let y = x. From Step 4, x ∈ S_i. From Step 3, x is integral over R_i. By the integrally closed condition stated in Step 5, this forces x ∈ R_i. Since this argument holds for every i, we have x ∈ R_i for all i.

7. By the definition of set intersection: an element belongs to ∩ R_i if and only if it belongs to R_i for every i. Since x ∈ R_i for all i (Step 6), we conclude x ∈ ∩ R_i.

8. We have demonstrated that whenever x ∈ ∩ S_i is integral over ∩ R_i, the element x must lie in ∩ R_i. Restating the definition of "integrally closed" applied to the pair (∩ R_i, ∩ S_i): ∩ R_i is integrally closed in ∩ S_i precisely when every element of ∩ S_i that is integral over ∩ R_i belongs to ∩ R_i. This condition is exactly what has been proved. ∎

9. Conclusion (the theorem statement): Let T be a commutative ring with identity and {S_i}, {R_i} families of subrings such that T is an extension ring of S_i and S_i is an extension ring of R_i for every i. If each R_i is integrally closed in S_i, then ∩ R_i is integrally closed in ∩ S_i. ∎
original_proof: Take any element x in ∩ S_i integral over ∩ R_i. Its monic polynomial has coefficients in ∩ R_i ⊆ each R_i, so x is integral over each R_i. Since R_i is integrally closed in S_i, x ∈ R_i for all i, hence x ∈ ∩ R_i.

name: Correspondence Between Contravariant and Covariant Functors
statement: Every contravariant functor S: C → D induces a unique covariant functor S̄: C^{op} → D defined by S̄(A)=S(A) and S̄(f^{op})=S(f). Conversely, every covariant functor on C^{op} arises uniquely from a contravariant functor on C.
gapfill_proof:
1. State the definition of contravariant functor. A contravariant functor S: C → D assigns to each object A ∈ C an object S(A) ∈ D, and to each morphism f: A → B in C a morphism S(f): S(B) → S(A) in D, satisfying (i) S(id_A) = id_{S(A)} and (ii) S(g ∘ f) = S(f) ∘ S(g) for all composable f: A → B, g: B → C in C.

2. State the definition of the opposite category C^{op}. The objects of C^{op} are exactly the objects of C. For each morphism f: A → B in C, there exists a morphism f^{op}: B → A in C^{op}. The identity on A in C^{op} is (id_A)^{op}. Composition in C^{op} is defined by f^{op} ∘_{op} g^{op} = (g ∘ f)^{op} where ∘ denotes composition in C.

3. Define the induced covariant functor S̄: C^{op} → D. On objects, set S̄(A) = S(A) for every A ∈ C^{op}. On morphisms, for each f^{op}: B → A in C^{op} (corresponding to f: A → B in C), set S̄(f^{op}) = S(f). The codomain of S̄(f^{op}) is S(A) = S̄(A) and the domain is S(B) = S̄(B), so S̄(f^{op}): S̄(B) → S̄(A) is a well-typed morphism in D.

4. Verify that S̄ preserves identities. Compute S̄(id_A in C^{op}) = S̄((id_A)^{op}) by the definition of identity in C^{op}. By the definition of S̄ on morphisms, S̄((id_A)^{op}) = S(id_A). Because S is a contravariant functor, it preserves identities: S(id_A) = id_{S(A)}. Since S̄(A) = S(A), we obtain S̄(id_A in C^{op}) = id_{S̄(A)}.

5. Verify that S̄ preserves composition. Let f^{op}: B → A and g^{op}: C → B be composable morphisms in C^{op}, corresponding to f: A → B and g: B → C in C. The composition in C^{op} is f^{op} ∘_{op} g^{op} = (g ∘ f)^{op}: C → A. Apply S̄: S̄(f^{op} ∘_{op} g^{op}) = S̄((g ∘ f)^{op}) = S(g ∘ f), using the definition of S̄ on morphisms.

6. Apply the contravariance property of S to S(g ∘ f). Since g: B → C and f: A → B, we have g ∘ f: A → C. By contravariance condition (ii), S(g ∘ f) = S(f) ∘ S(g). Substitute into the previous equality: S̄(f^{op} ∘_{op} g^{op}) = S(f) ∘ S(g).

7. Express the right-hand side in terms of S̄. By definition, S̄(f^{op}) = S(f) and S̄(g^{op}) = S(g). Therefore S(f) ∘ S(g) = S̄(f^{op}) ∘ S̄(g^{op}). Combining with Step 6 yields S̄(f^{op} ∘_{op} g^{op}) = S̄(f^{op}) ∘ S̄(g^{op}).

8. Conclude that S̄ is a covariant functor. Steps 4 and 7 together establish that S̄ preserves both identities and composition in C^{op}, which are the two axioms defining a covariant functor. Thus every contravariant functor S: C → D induces a covariant functor S̄: C^{op} → D.

9. Prove the converse direction. Let T: C^{op} → D be an arbitrary covariant functor. Define S: C → D by S(A) = T(A) on objects and S(f) = T(f^{op}) on morphisms, where f: A → B in C and f^{op}: B → A in C^{op}. Check well-typedness: T(f^{op}): T(B) → T(A), so S(f): S(B) → S(A), which reverses the direction of f, as required for a contravariant functor.

10. Verify that S preserves identities. Compute S(id_A) = T((id_A)^{op}) = T(id_A in C^{op}). Since T is a covariant functor, T(id_A in C^{op}) = id_{T(A)} = id_{S(A)}.

11. Verify that S reverses composition. Let f: A → B and g: B → C be composable in C. Compute S(g ∘ f) = T((g ∘ f)^{op}) = T(f^{op} ∘_{op} g^{op}), using the definition of opposite composition. Since T is covariant, T(f^{op} ∘_{op} g^{op}) = T(f^{op}) ∘ T(g^{op}) = S(f) ∘ S(g). Thus S(g ∘ f) = S(f) ∘ S(g), which is precisely the contravariance condition.

12. Prove the correspondence is a bijection. Starting from a contravariant functor S, constructing S̄ as in Step 3 and then applying the converse construction from Step 9 to S̄ recovers S: on objects, S̄(A) = S(A) and T(f^{op}) = S̄(f^{op}) = S(f), so the round-trip yields the original S. Conversely, starting from a covariant functor T on C^{op}, defining S as in Step 9 and then constructing S̄ as in Step 3 yields S̄(f^{op}) = S(f) = T(f^{op}), so the round-trip recovers T. The correspondence is one-to-one and onto.

13. State the final result. There exists a natural bijection between the class of contravariant functors from C to D and the class of covariant functors from C^{op} to D, given by the mutually inverse mappings S ↦ S̄ and T ↦ S_T defined above. ∎
original_proof: Verification of functor axioms using the definition of opposite category composition.

name: Functor of Several Variables (Mixed Variance)
statement: A functor T of two variables, contravariant in the first and covariant in the second, from C × D to E assigns an object T(C,D) and for each f: C→C', g: D→D' a morphism T(f,g): T(C',D) → T(C,D') satisfying T(1_C,1_D)=1_{T(C,D)} and T(f'∘f, g'∘g)=T(f,g')∘T(f',g).
original_proof: N/A - Definition

name: Maschke's Theorem
statement: Let G be a finite group and k a field. The group algebra k[G] is semisimple if and only if the characteristic of k does not divide the order of G.
gapfill_proof:
1. Let R = k[G] denote the group algebra of a finite group G over a field k. Recall the definition: R is the k-vector space with basis {e_g : g ∈ G} and multiplication given by e_g e_h = e_{gh}, extended k-bilinearly.

2. **Proof of the forward direction (⇒).** Assume char(k) ∤ |G|, i.e., |G| is invertible in k.

3. To prove R is semisimple, we prove that every left R-module is completely reducible. By definition, an R-module M is completely reducible if every R-submodule N ⊆ M is a direct summand of M (there exists an R-submodule P ⊆ M such that M = N ⊕ P). By a standard characterization, a ring is semisimple if and only if it is semisimple as a left module over itself, which is equivalent to the statement that every left R-module is completely reducible.

4. Let V be an arbitrary left k[G]-module, and let U ⊆ V be a k[G]-submodule. We will construct a k[G]-linear complement for U in V.

5. Regard V and U as k-vector spaces by restricting scalars from k[G] to k via the inclusion k ↪ k[G] (sending λ ∈ k to λ e_1 where 1 is the identity of G). Since every vector space has a basis (Axiom of Choice / Basis Extension Theorem), extend a k-basis of U to a k-basis of V.

6. By the Basis Extension Theorem, there exists a k-linear projection φ: V → U, i.e., a k-linear map satisfying φ(u) = u for all u ∈ U and im(φ) ⊆ U. Explicitly, for each basis vector v of V, set φ(v) = v if v belongs to the chosen basis of U, and φ(v) = 0 if v belongs to the complementing basis.

7. Define the Reynolds operator (averaging operator) ψ: V → V by the formula ψ(v) = |G|^{-1} · ∑_{g∈G} g·φ(g^{-1}·v), where the action g·w denotes the left module action of the basis element e_g ∈ k[G] on w ∈ V, and |G|^{-1} denotes the multiplicative inverse of |G| in the field k.

8. The scalar |G|^{-1} exists in k because char(k) ∤ |G|. By the definition of the characteristic of a field, this means the integer |G|, interpreted as |G|·1_k ∈ k, is non-zero, hence invertible in the field k.

9. The map ψ is k-linear: g·(−) and φ and g^{-1}·(−) are all k-linear maps, the sum of k-linear maps is k-linear, and scalar multiplication by |G|^{-1} preserves k-linearity.

10. The map ψ is k[G]-linear (G-equivariant). Verify: for any h ∈ G and v ∈ V, ψ(h·v) = |G|^{-1} ∑_{g∈G} g·φ(g^{-1}h·v). Perform the change of variables x = h^{-1}g, which ranges over G as g runs over G (since left multiplication by h^{-1} is a bijection on G). Then g = hx and g^{-1}h = x^{-1}h^{-1}h = x^{-1}. Thus ψ(h·v) = |G|^{-1} ∑_{x∈G} hx·φ(x^{-1}·v) = h · (|G|^{-1} ∑_{x∈G} x·φ(x^{-1}·v)) = h·ψ(v).

11. The image of ψ is contained in U. For any v ∈ V and g ∈ G, the element g^{-1}·v belongs to V, and φ maps it into U by the definition of φ (step 6). Since U is a k[G]-submodule, it is closed under the G-action, so g·φ(g^{-1}·v) ∈ U. The sum of elements of U is in U, and scaling by |G|^{-1} preserves membership in the subspace U. Hence ψ(v) ∈ U.

12. The map ψ restricts to the identity on U. For any u ∈ U and g ∈ G, the element g^{-1}·u belongs to U (since U is a submodule). Then φ(g^{-1}·u) = g^{-1}·u (since φ is a projection onto U, step 6). Hence g·φ(g^{-1}·u) = g·(g^{-1}·u) = (gg^{-1})·u = 1_G·u = u. Summing over all g ∈ G gives ∑_{g∈G} g·φ(g^{-1}·u) = ∑_{g∈G} u = |G|·u. Multiplying by |G|^{-1} yields ψ(u) = u.

13. Consequently, ψ: V → U is a k[G]-linear projection onto U (idempotent with image U). Its kernel K = ker(ψ) = {v ∈ V : ψ(v) = 0} is a k[G]-submodule of V, because the kernel of any k[G]-module homomorphism is a submodule.

14. By the First Isomorphism Theorem for modules (equivalently, the rank–nullity / dimension formula in linear algebra, but here applied to the k[G]-linear map ψ and the submodule U = im(ψ)), we obtain the direct sum decomposition V = U ⊕ K as k[G]-modules. Explicitly: every v ∈ V can be written as v = ψ(v) + (v − ψ(v)); the first term belongs to U, the second to K because ψ(v − ψ(v)) = ψ(v) − ψ(ψ(v)) = ψ(v) − ψ(v) = 0; if u ∈ U ∩ K then u = ψ(u) = 0; hence the sum is direct.

15. Since V and U were arbitrary, every submodule of every k[G]-module is a direct summand. By definition, every k[G]-module is completely reducible (semisimple). Therefore k[G] is a semisimple ring.

16. **Proof of the reverse direction (⇐).** Assume that char(k) divides |G|, i.e., |G| = 0 in the field k.

17. Consider the element z = ∑_{g∈G} e_g in the group algebra k[G], the sum of all standard basis elements. (This element is sometimes called the norm element or the averaging element of the group algebra.)

18. The element z is non-zero. The set {e_g : g ∈ G} is a k-basis of k[G] by the definition of the group algebra. In particular, the collection {e_g} is k-linearly independent. Since G is non-empty (it is a group, hence contains the identity), the coefficient 1 on each basis vector is non-zero in k, and a non-trivial linear combination of linearly independent vectors is non-zero. Hence z ≠ 0 in k[G].

19. The element z is central in k[G]. For any h ∈ G, compute h·z = e_h · ∑_{g∈G} e_g = ∑_{g∈G} e_{hg}. As g runs over the group G, the product hg also runs over all elements of G exactly once (left multiplication by h is a bijection on G). Hence ∑_{g∈G} e_{hg} = ∑_{x∈G} e_x = z. A symmetric computation using right multiplication shows z·e_h = z for all h ∈ G. Since the group elements {e_g : g ∈ G} generate k[G] as a k-vector space, z commutes with every element of k[G]. Thus z belongs to the center Z(k[G]).

20. Compute the square of z: z^2 = (∑_{g∈G} e_g) · (∑_{h∈G} e_h) = ∑_{g,h∈G} e_g e_h = ∑_{g,h∈G} e_{gh}.

21. Perform a re-indexing of the double sum. Fix an element s ∈ G and count how many pairs (g, h) ∈ G × G satisfy gh = s. For any g ∈ G, the equation gh = s determines h uniquely as h = g^{-1}s. Hence for each fixed s, there are exactly |G| pairs (g, h) with gh = s (one for each choice of g). Thus ∑_{g,h∈G} e_{gh} = ∑_{s∈G} |G| · e_s = |G| · ∑_{s∈G} e_s = |G| · z.

22. Since char(k) divides |G| by assumption, the integer |G|, when interpreted as |G|·1_k in the field k, equals 0_k. Therefore |G| · z = 0_k · z = 0 in k[G]. From step 21, this yields z^2 = 0.

23. Consider the principal (two-sided) ideal I generated by z in k[G]: I = k[G] · z · k[G]. Because z is central (step 19), this two-sided ideal simplifies to the left ideal I = k[G]z = {a z : a ∈ k[G]}. Since z is non-zero (step 18) and k[G] has a unit 1 (the basis element e_1 corresponding to the identity group element), the element 1·z = z is a non-zero element of I. Hence I ≠ (0).

24. The ideal I is nilpotent. Specifically, I^2 = 0. Take any two elements a z, b z ∈ I with a, b ∈ k[G]. Compute (a z)(b z) = a (z b) z = a (b z) z (since z is central) = a b z^2 = a b · 0 = 0 (using z^2 = 0 from step 22). Hence the product of any two elements of I is zero; consequently I^2 = (0). Thus I is a non-zero nilpotent two-sided ideal.

25. Recall the definition of the Jacobson radical rad(R) of a ring R: it is the intersection of all maximal left ideals of R. A fundamental theorem of ring theory states that the Jacobson radical contains every nilpotent one-sided ideal. (Proof: if J is a nilpotent left ideal, say J^n = 0, then for any maximal left ideal M of R, either J ⊆ M or M + J = R; the latter case leads to a contradiction using nilpotence, forcing J ⊆ M.) Therefore I ⊆ rad(k[G]).

26. For a semisimple ring R, the Jacobson radical is zero: rad(R) = (0). This is a defining property of semisimple rings: a ring is semisimple (in the sense of being a direct sum of simple left modules) if and only if it is left Artinian and has zero Jacobson radical (Wedderburn–Artin Theorem). In particular, any ring with a non-zero nilpotent ideal cannot be semisimple.

27. Since k[G] contains the non-zero nilpotent ideal I, its Jacobson radical is non-zero (I ⊆ rad(k[G])), contradicting the requirement rad(k[G]) = 0 for semisimplicity. Therefore k[G] is not a semisimple ring.

28. Combining the forward direction (steps 2–15: char(k) ∤ |G| ⇒ k[G] semisimple) and the reverse direction (steps 16–27: char(k) | |G| ⇒ k[G] not semisimple), we conclude: the group algebra k[G] is semisimple if and only if the characteristic of k does not divide the order of G. ∎
original_proof: If char(k) ∤ |G|, prove every k[G]-module is completely reducible by averaging a linear projection over the group action using the Reynolds operator. The converse is shown by considering the sum of all group elements.

name: Noether Normalization Lemma
statement: Let A be a finitely generated algebra over a field k. Then there exist algebraically independent elements y₁,…,y_d ∈ A such that A is a finitely generated module over the polynomial subring k[y₁,…,y_d].
gapfill_proof:
1. Write A = k[x₁, …, x_n] where x₁, …, x_n are a finite set of generators of A over the field k, invoking the definition of Finitely Generated Algebra.
2. Proceed by induction on the number of generators n.
3. Case 1 (base of induction): x₁, …, x_n are algebraically independent over k. By the definition of Algebraically Independent elements, no nonzero polynomial in n variables over k vanishes at (x₁, …, x_n). Set d = n and y_i = x_i for i = 1, …, n. The subring k[y₁, …, y_d] equals A, and A is a free module of rank 1 over itself, which is finitely generated by the single generator 1. The statement holds.
4. Case 2 (induction step): x₁, …, x_n are algebraically dependent over k. By definition, there exists a nonzero polynomial f ∈ k[t₁, …, t_n] such that f(x₁, …, x_n) = 0 in A.
5. Write f as a sum of monomials: f = Σ_{α} c_{α} · t₁^{a₁} t₂^{a₂} ⋯ t_n^{a_n}, where each c_{α} ∈ k and only finitely many coefficients are nonzero.
6. Let D be the maximum exponent appearing in any monomial of f: D = max{ a_i : α = (a₁, …, a_n) with c_{α} ≠ 0, i = 1, …, n }.
7. Choose an integer d satisfying d > D. For each i = 1, …, n−1, define the integer r_i = d^i.
8. For each i = 1, …, n−1, define the new element z_i = x_i − x_n^{r_i} in A. Express each original generator as x_i = z_i + x_n^{r_i}.
9. Substitute these expressions into the generation set: A = k[x₁, …, x_n] = k[z₁, …, z_{n−1}, x_n], because each x_i is a polynomial in z_i and x_n, and conversely each z_i is a polynomial in x_i and x_n.
10. Substitute t_i = z_i + x_n^{r_i} for i = 1, …, n−1 and t_n = x_n into the polynomial f. Obtain the identity g(z₁, …, z_{n−1}, x_n) := f(z₁ + x_n^{r₁}, …, z_{n−1} + x_n^{r_{n-1}}, x_n) = 0.
11. Consider a single monomial c_{α} · t₁^{a₁} ⋯ t_n^{a_n} of f. Expand (z_i + x_n^{r_i})^{a_i} using the Binomial Theorem. The term of highest degree in x_n within this factor is x_n^{a_i r_i}.
12. The sum of the highest-degree contributions across all factors i = 1, …, n−1 and the factor t_n^{a_n} = x_n^{a_n} yields the exponent w(α) = a_n + Σ_{i=1}^{n−1} a_i · r_i = a_n + Σ_{i=1}^{n−1} a_i · d^i.
13. Because each a_i satisfies 0 ≤ a_i ≤ D < d, the integer w(α) is precisely the number whose base-d digits are a_{α} = a_n, a_2 = a_{n−1}, …, a_n = a₁ (reading from least significant to most significant). The uniqueness of base-d representation implies that the map α ↦ w(α) is injective on the finite set of exponent vectors with c_{α} ≠ 0.
14. Among the finite set of exponent vectors appearing in f, select the unique vector α* that maximizes the weight w(α). Its coefficient c* := c_{α*} is nonzero in k.
15. Expand g by expanding each substituted monomial. The term in g arising from the monomial indexed by α* contributes c* · x_n^{w(α*)}. Every other monomial of f, having strictly smaller weight, produces terms in g whose x_n-degree is strictly less than w(α*).
16. Collecting terms in g by their degree in x_n, write g = c* · x_n^{w(α*)} + h(z₁, …, z_{n−1}, x_n), where h is a polynomial with coefficients in k[z₁, …, z_{n−1}] and degree in x_n strictly less than w(α*).
17. Since g = 0 and c* ≠ 0 in the field k, multiply the equation by (c*)⁻¹ and rearrange to obtain x_n^{w(α*)} = −(c*)⁻¹ · h(z₁, …, z_{n−1}, x_n).
18. This equation is a monic polynomial relation for x_n over the subring B := k[z₁, …, z_{n−1}]. By the definition of Integral Element, x_n is integral over B.
19. Invoke the lemma Integrality Implies Finite Generation: if an element s of a ring extension is integral over a subring R, then R[s] is a finitely generated R-module, with generating set {1, s, s², …, s^{m−1}} where m is the degree of the monic integral equation.
20. Applying this lemma with R = B and s = x_n, conclude that A = B[x_n] = k[z₁, …, z_{n−1}][x_n] is a finitely generated module over B.
21. The subring B = k[z₁, …, z_{n−1}] is a k-algebra generated by n−1 elements. Apply the induction hypothesis to B: there exist algebraically independent elements y₁, …, y_d ∈ B such that B is a finitely generated module over k[y₁, …, y_d].
22. The relation “finitely generated module” is transitive (Lemma: Transitivity of Finite Generation): if M is a finitely generated A-module and A is a finitely generated B-module, then M is a finitely generated B-module.
23. Apply transitivity with M = A, the intermediate ring B, and the base ring k[y₁, …, y_d]. Since A is a finitely generated B-module and B is a finitely generated k[y₁, …, y_d]-module, A is a finitely generated module over k[y₁, …, y_d].
24. The elements y₁, …, y_d are algebraically independent over k by the induction hypothesis, and they belong to B ⊆ A. The statement of the Noether Normalization Lemma is proved.
∎
original_proof: Induct on the number of generators. If the generators are algebraically dependent, replace them to lower the total degree of the relation, eventually obtaining algebraically independent elements over which A is finite.
