name: Cauchy's Theorem (Group Theory)
statement: If G is a finite group and p is a prime dividing the order of G, then G contains an element of order p.
gapfill_proof:
1. Define the set S = {(a_1, a_2, ..., a_p) ∈ G^p : a_1 a_2 ... a_p = e}, where e is the identity element of G. This constructs the domain on which the group action will operate.
2. Compute the cardinality of S. For any choice of the first p−1 components a_1, ..., a_{p−1} ∈ G (each independently chosen), the product condition forces a_p = (a_1 a_2 ... a_{p−1})^{-1}. By the Group Axiom of Inverses, this inverse exists and is unique.
3. By the Multiplication Principle of combinatorics, |S| = |G|^{p−1} · 1 = |G|^{p−1}, since there are |G| choices for each of the first p−1 coordinates and exactly one choice for a_p.
4. The hypothesis states that the prime p divides |G|. From the definition of divisibility, if p | |G| then p | |G|^{k} for every positive integer k. Taking k = p−1 yields p | |G|^{p−1} = |S|.
5. Let C_p = ⟨σ⟩ be the cyclic group of order p with generator σ. Define a map C_p × S → S by σ · (a_1, a_2, ..., a_p) = (a_2, a_3, ..., a_p, a_1). This is the cyclic left-shift action.
6. Verify that the map in step 5 satisfies the two Group Action Axioms: (i) e · s = s for all s ∈ S (the identity of C_p, namely σ^0, fixes every tuple); (ii) σ^i · (σ^j · s) = σ^{i+j} · s for all i, j and all s ∈ S, because applying two cyclic shifts sequentially is equivalent to a single shift by the sum of the exponents.
7. To confirm the action is well-defined, check that for any s = (a_1, ..., a_p) ∈ S, the image σ · s also belongs to S. The product of the components of σ · s is a_2 a_3 ... a_p a_1. By the Group Associativity Axiom, this equals (a_2 ... a_p)(a_1) = (a_1^{-1})(a_1) = e, where the equality a_2 ... a_p = a_1^{-1} follows from inverting both sides of the defining equation a_1 a_2 ... a_p = e and applying the Group Axiom of Inverses.
8. For any s ∈ S, let Orb(s) = {σ^i · s : 0 ≤ i < p} be the orbit of s under the action. The Orbit-Stabilizer Theorem states |Orb(s)| · |Stab(s)| = |C_p| = p, where Stab(s) is the stabilizer subgroup. Therefore |Orb(s)| divides p.
9. Since p is prime, the Definition of a Prime Number implies that the only positive divisors of p are 1 and p. Hence |Orb(s)| ∈ {1, p} for every s ∈ S.
10. The Orbit Partition Theorem asserts that the distinct orbits under a group action form a partition of the set S. Therefore |S| = Σ_{orbits O} |O|, where the sum extends over all distinct orbits.
11. Group the orbit sum by orbit size. Let Fix = {s ∈ S : σ · s = s} be the set of fixed points, i.e., points whose orbit has size 1. Let k be the number of orbits of size p. Then |S| = |Fix| · 1 + k · p = |Fix| + kp.
12. Reduce the equality |S| = |Fix| + kp modulo p. Since kp ≡ 0 (mod p) by the Definition of Modular Congruence, the result is |S| ≡ |Fix| (mod p).
13. Characterize the fixed points. A tuple (a_1, ..., a_p) ∈ S satisfies σ·s = s if and only if (a_2, a_3, ..., a_p, a_1) = (a_1, a_2, ..., a_p). Equating components yields a_1 = a_2, a_2 = a_3, ..., a_{p−1} = a_p, a_p = a_1, which forces a_1 = a_2 = ... = a_p. Call this common value a.
14. Substitute a_i = a for all i into the defining condition of S. The product condition a_1 a_2 ... a_p = e becomes a · a · ... · a (p factors) = a^p = e, using the Definition of Group Exponentiation.
15. Conversely, every element a ∈ G satisfying a^p = e produces the p-tuple (a, a, ..., a) ∈ S, and this tuple is fixed by σ. Thus the map φ: {a ∈ G : a^p = e} → Fix defined by φ(a) = (a, a, ..., a) is a bijection.
16. The identity element e ∈ G satisfies e^p = e by the Definition of the Identity Element (e^k = e for every positive integer k). Hence (e, e, ..., e) ∈ Fix, proving |Fix| ≥ 1.
17. From step 4, p divides |S|, which is equivalent to |S| ≡ 0 (mod p). From step 12, |S| ≡ |Fix| (mod p). By Transitivity of Modular Congruence, |Fix| ≡ 0 (mod p), meaning p divides |Fix|.
18. A positive integer divisible by the prime p ≥ 2 must be at least p. Since |Fix| is a positive integer (step 16) and p | |Fix| (step 17), the Ordering Property of Natural Numbers yields |Fix| ≥ p ≥ 2.
19. If the only element of G satisfying a^p = e were the identity e, then by the bijection from step 15, Fix would contain only the tuple (e, e, ..., e), giving |Fix| = 1. This contradicts |Fix| ≥ p ≥ 2 from step 18. Therefore there exists at least one element a ∈ G with a ≠ e and a^p = e.
20. For this element a, the Order Divisibility Lemma states: if a^n = e for some positive integer n, then the order of a divides n. Apply this lemma with n = p to the equality a^p = e, concluding that ord(a) divides the prime p.
21. By the Definition of Element Order, ord(a) is the smallest positive integer m such that a^m = e. Since ord(a) divides p and p is prime, ord(a) ∈ {1, p}. Because a ≠ e, the identity is the unique element of order 1, so ord(a) ≠ 1. Hence ord(a) = p.
22. Conclusion: Every finite group G whose order is divisible by a prime p contains at least one element of order p. ∎
original_proof: Consider the set of p-tuples of elements of G whose product is the identity. The cyclic group of order p acts on this set by cyclic permutation. The size of the set is |G|^(p-1), divisible by p. The fixed points under the action correspond to elements of order dividing p. By the class equation, the number of fixed points is congruent to the size of the set mod p, hence divisible by p. Since the identity is a fixed point, there must be nontrivial fixed points, giving an element of order p.

name: Trivial and Proper Subgroups
statement: The subgroup {1_G} is called the trivial subgroup (denoted 1). Any subgroup H ≠ G is a proper subgroup, written H < G.

name: Order of a Group
statement: The order of a group is the cardinality of its underlying set G, denoted |G|.

name: Generalized Associative Law
statement: If an element of a group is constructed from a sequence x₁, x₂, …, xₙ in order by repeatedly inserting brackets and applying the operation, the element must equal (…((x₁ ∘ x₂) ∘ x₃)…) ∘ xₙ, independently of the bracketing.
gapfill_proof:
1. Let G be a group with operation ∘. Define the right-associated product Πʳ recursively on the number of factors: for a single factor a, set Πʳ(a) = a; for m ≥ 2 factors, set Πʳ(a₁, a₂, …, aₘ) = Πʳ(a₁, …, aₘ₋₁) ∘ aₘ. The Generalized Associative Law asserts that for every integer n ≥ 1 and every sequence x₁, x₂, …, xₙ of elements of G, any element obtained from this sequence by repeatedly inserting brackets and applying ∘ equals Πʳ(x₁, …, xₙ). We prove this claim by induction on n.

2. Base case n = 1. The only element constructible from the singleton sequence x₁ is x₁ itself. By the definition of Πʳ for a single factor (step 1), Πʳ(x₁) = x₁. Hence the equality u = Πʳ(x₁) holds trivially for any bracketed product u of one element.

3. Base case n = 2. Any valid insertion of brackets into the sequence x₁, x₂ produces exactly the element x₁ ∘ x₂. By the recursive definition of Πʳ for m = 2 (step 1), Πʳ(x₁, x₂) = Πʳ(x₁) ∘ x₂ = x₁ ∘ x₂. Hence the claim holds for n = 2.

4. Induction hypothesis (strong induction). Assume that for every integer k with 1 ≤ k < n, where n ≥ 3, every element obtained from any sequence of k elements of G by any valid bracketing equals the right-associated product of those k elements. This is the standard Strong Induction Principle applied to the natural numbers.

5. Let u be an element of G obtained from the sequence x₁, x₂, …, xₙ by an arbitrary valid bracketing. Because n ≥ 3, the bracketing contains at least one application of the binary operation ∘, and the outermost such application partitions the sequence into two contiguous sub-sequences. By the definition of a valid bracketing as a full binary tree with leaves labeled x₁, …, xₙ in order, there exists an index i with 1 ≤ i < n, an element v obtained from the prefix x₁, …, xᵢ by a valid bracketing, and an element w obtained from the suffix xᵢ₊₁, …, xₙ by a valid bracketing, such that u = v ∘ w.

6. The sub-sequence for v has length i. Since 1 ≤ i < n, we have i < n. By the induction hypothesis (step 4) applied to the sequence x₁, …, xᵢ, the element v equals the right-associated product: v = Πʳ(x₁, …, xᵢ).

7. The sub-sequence for w has length n − i. Since i ≥ 1, we have n − i ≤ n − 1 < n. By the induction hypothesis (step 4) applied to the sequence xᵢ₊₁, …, xₙ, the element w equals the right-associated product: w = Πʳ(xᵢ₊₁, …, xₙ).

8. We now distinguish two mutually exclusive cases based on the length n − i of w.

9. Case A: n − i = 1, which is equivalent to i = n − 1. Then w is obtained from the single-element sequence xₙ. By the definition of Πʳ for a single factor (step 1), Πʳ(xₙ) = xₙ, so w = xₙ. Substituting the results for v and w into u: u = v ∘ w = Πʳ(x₁, …, xₙ₋₁) ∘ xₙ. By the recursive definition of Πʳ for m = n ≥ 2 (step 1), Πʳ(x₁, …, xₙ₋₁) ∘ xₙ is exactly Πʳ(x₁, …, xₙ). The claim holds in Case A.

10. Case B: n − i ≥ 2, which is equivalent to i ≤ n − 2. The sequence xᵢ₊₁, …, xₙ has length at least 2. Apply the recursive clause of the definition of Πʳ (step 1, case m ≥ 2) to this sequence: Πʳ(xᵢ₊₁, …, xₙ) = Πʳ(xᵢ₊₁, …, xₙ₋₁) ∘ xₙ. Since w = Πʳ(xᵢ₊₁, …, xₙ) by step 7, we obtain w = Πʳ(xᵢ₊₁, …, xₙ₋₁) ∘ xₙ.

11. Substitute the expressions for v (from step 6) and w (from step 10) into u = v ∘ w: u = Πʳ(x₁, …, xᵢ) ∘ (Πʳ(xᵢ₊₁, …, xₙ₋₁) ∘ xₙ).

12. Apply the Associativity Axiom of the group G. For all elements a, b, c ∈ G, the axiom states that (a ∘ b) ∘ c = a ∘ (b ∘ c). Read this identity from right to left with the substitution a = Πʳ(x₁, …, xᵢ), b = Πʳ(xᵢ₊₁, …, xₙ₋₁), c = xₙ: a ∘ (b ∘ c) = (a ∘ b) ∘ c. The right-hand side of u matches a ∘ (b ∘ c). Therefore, u = (Πʳ(x₁, …, xᵢ) ∘ Πʳ(xᵢ₊₁, …, xₙ₋₁)) ∘ xₙ.

13. The subexpression Πʳ(x₁, …, xᵢ) ∘ Πʳ(xᵢ₊₁, …, xₙ₋₁) is an element obtained from the sequence x₁, …, xₙ₋₁ by a valid bracketing: the first i elements are combined via their right-associated product Πʳ(x₁, …, xᵢ), the next (n − 1 − i) elements are combined via their right-associated product Πʳ(xᵢ₊₁, …, xₙ₋₁), and the two results are joined by the operation ∘. This sequence has length n − 1. Since n ≥ 3, we have n − 1 ≥ 2, and in any case n − 1 < n. By the induction hypothesis (step 4) applied to this bracketed product of n − 1 elements: Πʳ(x₁, …, xᵢ) ∘ Πʳ(xᵢ₊₁, …, xₙ₋₁) = Πʳ(x₁, …, xₙ₋₁).

14. Substitute the equality from step 13 into the expression for u from step 12: u = Πʳ(x₁, …, xₙ₋₁) ∘ xₙ. By the recursive definition of Πʳ for m = n ≥ 2 (step 1), Πʳ(x₁, …, xₙ₋₁) ∘ xₙ is exactly Πʳ(x₁, …, xₙ). The claim holds in Case B.

15. Both Case A (step 9) and Case B (steps 10–14) yield the same conclusion: the arbitrarily bracketed element u equals the right-associated product Πʳ(x₁, …, xₙ). The induction step is complete. By the Principle of Mathematical Induction, the equality u = Πʳ(x₁, …, xₙ) holds for every integer n ≥ 1 and every sequence of n group elements. Consequently, all bracketings of a given ordered sequence of group elements produce the same value, which is the content of the Generalized Associative Law.
∎
original_proof: Induction on n. Write u = v ∘ w with v from x₁…xᵢ, w from xᵢ₊₁…xₙ. If w = xₙ, induction applies directly. Else w = w' ∘ xₙ, then u = (v ∘ w') ∘ xₙ and induction gives the result.

name: Ω-Direct Factor and Indecomposability
statement: Let G be a group with operator domain Ω. An Ω-subgroup H is called an Ω-direct factor of G if there exists an Ω-subgroup K such that G = H × K; K is an Ω-direct complement of H. If G has no proper nontrivial Ω-direct factors, G is Ω-indecomposable (or indecomposable if Ω = ∅).
original_proof: None (definition).

name: Soluble Word Problem
statement: For a finite presentation, the word problem is soluble if there is an algorithm that, given a word w in the generators, decides whether w is a relator (i.e., w = 1 in the group). A group G has soluble word problem if the word problem is soluble for some (and hence any) finite presentation of G.

name: Definition of Group-Theoretical Class and Property
statement: A group-theoretical class X is a class of groups such that (i) X contains a group of order 1, and (ii) if G_1 ≅ G ∈ X then G_1 ∈ X. A group-theoretical property is a property of groups such that a group of order 1 has it, and if a group has it, so does any isomorphic copy. There is a natural bijection between group-theoretical properties and group-theoretical classes.

name: Sylow's Theorems
statement: Let G be a finite group of order p^a * m where p is prime and p does not divide m. Then (i) Every p-subgroup of G is contained in a subgroup of order p^a; in particular, Sylow p-subgroups always exist. (ii) If n_p is the number of Sylow p-subgroups, n_p ≡ 1 mod p. (iii) All Sylow p-subgroups are conjugate in G.
gapfill_proof:
1. Let G be a finite group. Write |G| = p^a · m where p is a prime, a ≥ 0, and p ∤ m.

2. If a = 0 then the trivial subgroup {1} is the unique Sylow p-subgroup, n_p = 1 ≡ 1 (mod p), and (i)-(iii) hold trivially. Assume a ≥ 1 for the remainder.

3. --- Part (i): Existence of Sylow p-subgroups ---

4. Define X = {S ⊆ G : |S| = p^a}, the collection of all subsets of G whose cardinality equals p^a.

5. The mapping G × X → X given by (g, S) ↦ Sg = {sg : s ∈ S} defines a right action of G on X.

6. The cardinality of X is the binomial coefficient |X| = C(|G|, p^a) = C(p^a m, p^a).

7. Expand (1 + T)^{p^a m} modulo p using the Freshman's Dream identity (1 + T)^{p^a} ≡ 1 + T^{p^a} (mod p), which holds because p divides all binomial coefficients C(p^a, k) for 0 < k < p^a (Lucas's Theorem). Iterating gives (1 + T)^{p^a m} ≡ (1 + T^{p^a})^m (mod p).

8. The coefficient of T^{p^a} in (1 + T)^{p^a m} is C(p^a m, p^a). The coefficient of T^{p^a} in (1 + T^{p^a})^m is C(m, 1) = m.

9. Since the two polynomials are congruent modulo p, their coefficients satisfy C(p^a m, p^a) ≡ m (mod p).

10. By hypothesis p ∤ m, therefore p ∤ C(p^a m, p^a). Conclude p ∤ |X|.

11. Decompose X into disjoint G-orbits: |X| = Σ_{i} |O(S_i)|, where each S_i is an orbit representative and |O(S_i)| = [G : Stab_G(S_i)] by the Orbit-Stabilizer Theorem.

12. Since p ∤ |X|, at least one orbit O(S_0) satisfies p ∤ |O(S_0)|.

13. Set P = Stab_G(S_0) = {g ∈ G : S_0 g = S_0}. By the Orbit-Stabilizer Theorem, [G : P] = |O(S_0)|.

14. Since p ∤ [G : P] and |G| = [G : P] · |P| = p^a m, the p-adic valuation satisfies v_p(|P|) ≥ a. Equivalently, p^a divides |P|.

15. The subset S_0 is a union of right cosets of P: for any s ∈ S_0, the right coset sP = {sg : g ∈ P} is contained in S_0 because S_0 P = S_0.

16. Therefore |S_0| = p^a is a multiple of |P|. Write p^a = k · |P| for some integer k ≥ 1.

17. The integer |P| divides p^a, so |P| = p^b for some b ≤ a (by uniqueness of prime factorization).

18. From step 14, p^a divides |P|, and from step 17, |P| divides p^a. Hence |P| = p^a.

19. The subgroup P is a Sylow p-subgroup of G. This establishes existence of Sylow p-subgroups.

20. Now let Q be any p-subgroup of G. Consider the left coset space G/P and the action of Q on G/P by left multiplication: q · (gP) = (qg)P.

21. Decompose G/P into Q-orbits. By the Orbit-Stabilizer Theorem, each orbit has size dividing |Q|, which is a power of p.

22. The total size |G/P| = [G : P] = m satisfies p ∤ m (by definition of m).

23. If every Q-orbit had size divisible by p, then the sum |G/P| would be divisible by p, contradicting step 22. Therefore some Q-orbit has size 1 (the only p-power not divisible by p is 1).

24. A size-1 orbit is a fixed point: there exists g ∈ G such that Q · gP = {gP}, i.e., qgP = gP for all q ∈ Q.

25. The condition qgP = gP is equivalent to g^{-1}qg ∈ P for all q ∈ Q, i.e., g^{-1}Qg ⊆ P.

26. The conjugate g^{-1}Qg is a subgroup of P, and |P| = p^a. Thus Q is contained in a subgroup of order p^a. This proves that every p-subgroup lies in some Sylow p-subgroup, completing part (i).

27. --- Part (ii): Congruence n_p ≡ 1 mod p ---

28. Fix a Sylow p-subgroup P of G (whose existence was proved in part (i)).

29. Define Syl_p(G) = {gPg^{-1} : g ∈ G}, the set of all Sylow p-subgroups of G (conjugates of P). Set n_p = |Syl_p(G)|.

30. Let P act on Syl_p(G) by conjugation: for x ∈ P and Q ∈ Syl_p(G), define x · Q = xQx^{-1}.

31. Decompose Syl_p(G) into P-orbits. By the Orbit-Stabilizer Theorem, each P-orbit has size dividing |P| = p^a.

32. Suppose Q ∈ Syl_p(G) satisfies that its P-orbit has size 1. Then xQx^{-1} = Q for all x ∈ P, which means P ⊆ N_G(Q) (P is contained in the normalizer of Q).

33. Form the subgroup PQ = {xy : x ∈ P, y ∈ Q} of G. Since P ⊆ N_G(Q), the product set PQ is a subgroup by the normalizer criterion, and |PQ| = |P| · |Q| / |P ∩ Q|.

34. Both |P| = p^a and |Q| = p^a are powers of p. The integer |P ∩ Q| is also a power of p as a subgroup of both P and Q. Therefore |PQ| = p^{2a} / p^c = p^{2a-c} for some integer c.

35. The product PQ contains P as a subgroup, so |P| ≤ |PQ|, giving p^a ≤ p^{2a-c}. But since both P and Q are Sylow p-subgroups, |P| is the maximal p-power dividing |G|, hence |PQ| ≤ p^a.

36. From p^{2a-c} ≤ p^a we obtain 2a - c ≤ a, so a ≤ c, meaning c ≥ a. Since P ∩ Q ⊆ P and |P| = p^a, we have c ≤ a. Thus c = a.

37. With c = a, the equality 2a - c = a gives |PQ| = p^a = |P|. Since P ⊆ PQ, conclude P = PQ. Similarly Q ⊆ PQ = P, so Q = P.

38. Therefore the unique P-orbit of size 1 in Syl_p(G) is {P}.

39. All other P-orbits have size a positive power of p (since orbit sizes divide |P| = p^a and are not 1). Every such size is divisible by p.

40. Summing orbit sizes: n_p = |Syl_p(G)| = 1 + (sum of sizes divisible by p). Reducing modulo p yields n_p ≡ 1 (mod p). This proves part (ii).

41. --- Part (iii): Conjugacy of Sylow p-subgroups ---

42. Let P be a Sylow p-subgroup of G and let Q be any p-subgroup of G.

43. Let Q act on Syl_p(G) by conjugation: for y ∈ Q and R ∈ Syl_p(G), define y · R = yRy^{-1}.

44. Decompose Syl_p(G) into Q-orbits. By the Orbit-Stabilizer Theorem, each Q-orbit has size dividing |Q|, which is a power of p.

45. Suppose, for contradiction, that no Q-orbit has size 1. Then every Q-orbit has size divisible by p. Summing would give n_p ≡ 0 (mod p).

46. But part (ii) proved n_p ≡ 1 (mod p). The congruence 1 ≡ 0 (mod p) is impossible for a prime p. This contradiction refutes the supposition in step 45.

47. Therefore Q has at least one fixed point in Syl_p(G): there exists R ∈ Syl_p(G) such that yRy^{-1} = R for all y ∈ Q. Equivalently, Q ⊆ N_G(R).

48. Apply the argument of steps 33-36 to the pair (Q, R). The product QR is a p-subgroup of G containing R. Since R is a Sylow p-subgroup and thus maximal, QR = R, so Q ⊆ R.

49. Since R is a conjugate of P (by definition of Syl_p(G)), write R = gPg^{-1} for some g ∈ G. Then Q ⊆ gPg^{-1}, i.e., g^{-1}Qg ⊆ P.

50. Taking Q to be another Sylow p-subgroup P′ in step 49 gives g^{-1}P′g ⊆ P. Comparing orders: |g^{-1}P′g| = |P′| = p^a = |P|, so the inclusion is equality: g^{-1}P′g = P, hence P′ = gPg^{-1}.

51. Therefore any two Sylow p-subgroups are conjugate in G. Moreover, every p-subgroup of G is contained in a conjugate of P. This completes part (iii).

52. ∎
original_proof: Let X be the set of all subsets of G of size p^a. G acts on X by right multiplication. Show p does not divide |X| = C(p^a m, p^a) by examining powers of p in numerator and denominator. Hence there exists a G-orbit whose size is not divisible by p. For an element in this orbit, its stabilizer P has index not divisible by p, and its order divides p^a and is at least p^a, so |P|=p^a; thus a Sylow p-subgroup exists. For (ii), let P be a Sylow p-subgroup and let 𝒮 be the set of its conjugates. P acts on 𝒮 by conjugation; orbits have p-power size. If {P1} is an orbit of size 1, then P ≤ N_G(P1), PP1 is a p-group containing P, so P=P1. Thus {P} is the unique orbit of size 1, giving n_p ≡ 1 mod p. For (iii), if P2 is a p-subgroup not contained in any conjugate of P, then P2 acts on 𝒮; any P2-orbit would have size >1 (otherwise P2 ≤ P3), so all orbit sizes are divisible by p, making n_p ≡ 0 mod p, contradicting (ii). Hence every p-subgroup lies in a conjugate of P; in particular all Sylow p-subgroups are conjugate.

name: Metabelian Group
statement: A soluble group with derived length at most 2 is called metabelian.

name: Nilpotent Extension Property
statement: Let P be a property of groups that is closed under forming extensions. If G is a nilpotent group and its abelianization G_ab has P, then G itself has P.
gapfill_proof:
1. By the definition of a nilpotent group (P. Hall, "A Contribution to the Theory of Groups of Prime-Power Order", 1934), the lower central series of G is the descending chain γ_1(G) ⊇ γ_2(G) ⊇ … defined by γ_1(G) = G and γ_{i+1}(G) = [γ_i(G), G] = ⟨ x^{-1}g^{-1}xg : x ∈ γ_i(G), g ∈ G ⟩, and G is nilpotent if and only if there exists an integer c ≥ 0 such that γ_{c+1}(G) = {1}.

2. Fix the nilpotency class c of G, so that γ_{c+1}(G) = {1} and γ_c(G) ≠ {1} (unless c = 0, in which case G = {1} and the statement holds trivially because the trivial group is the extension of itself by itself, so {1} ∈ P). Assume c ≥ 1 for the remainder of the proof.

3. For each i ∈ {1, 2, …, c}, define the i-th lower central factor F_i = γ_i(G) / γ_{i+1}(G). By the definition of the commutator subgroup, γ_{i+1}(G) = [γ_i(G), G] is a normal subgroup of γ_i(G), so the quotient F_i is well-defined.

4. The subgroup γ_{i+1}(G) contains the derived subgroup of γ_i(G), because [γ_i(G), γ_i(G)] ≤ [γ_i(G), G] = γ_{i+1}(G). Hence F_i = γ_i(G) / γ_{i+1}(G) is an abelian group for each i.

5. The abelianization of G is defined as G_ab = G / [G, G] = G / γ_2(G) = F_1. The hypothesis of the theorem states that G_ab possesses property P. Therefore F_1 has property P.

6. Claim: For every index i ∈ {1, 2, …, c}, the lower central factor F_i has property P.

7. Proof of the claim by induction on i. Base case (i = 1): Step 5 established that F_1 = G_ab has property P.

8. Induction hypothesis: Assume F_k has property P for some k with 1 ≤ k < c. We shall prove that F_{k+1} has property P.

9. Consider the commutator map [,] : γ_k(G) × G → γ_{k+1}(G) defined by [x, g] = x^{-1}g^{-1}xg. This map is surjective onto γ_{k+1}(G) by the definition γ_{k+1}(G) = [γ_k(G), G], since every element of γ_{k+1}(G) is a product of commutators of the form [x, g] with x ∈ γ_k(G) and g ∈ G.

10. By the Hall commutator identity [γ_k(G), γ_2(G)] ≤ γ_{k+2}(G) (P. Hall, "A Contribution to the Theory of Groups of Prime-Power Order", Theorem 3.1), the commutator of any element of γ_k(G) with any element of γ_2(G) lies in γ_{k+2}(G). Likewise, [γ_{k+1}(G), G] = γ_{k+2}(G) by definition of the lower central series. These two inclusions ensure that the commutator map factors through the quotients, inducing a well-defined map β : F_k × G_ab → F_{k+1} given by β(x·γ_{k+1}(G), g·γ_2(G)) = [x, g]·γ_{k+2}(G).

11. The map β is bilinear in its two arguments: for fixed x·γ_{k+1}(G) ∈ F_k, the map g·γ_2(G) ↦ [x, g]·γ_{k+2}(G) is a group homomorphism because [x, gh] = [x, h]·[x, g]^h ≡ [x, h]·[x, g] (mod γ_{k+2}(G)) by the commutator expansion formula; analogously for the first argument.

12. Form the tensor product of abelian groups F_k ⊗_ℤ G_ab over the ring of integers ℤ. By the universal property of the tensor product (S. Lang, "Algebra", 3rd ed., Chapter XVI, §1, Theorem 1.1), the bilinear map β factors uniquely through a ℤ-linear homomorphism φ : F_k ⊗_ℤ G_ab → F_{k+1} satisfying φ((x·γ_{k+1}(G)) ⊗ (g·γ_2(G))) = [x, g]·γ_{k+2}(G).

13. The homomorphism φ is surjective: every element of F_{k+1} = γ_{k+1}(G)/γ_{k+2}(G) is a coset h·γ_{k+2}(G) with h ∈ γ_{k+1}(G) = [γ_k(G), G], so h is a product of commutators [x_j, g_j]; the image of each elementary tensor (x_j·γ_{k+1}(G)) ⊗ (g_j·γ_2(G)) under φ is exactly the coset of that commutator in F_{k+1}, and φ extends linearly to cover the entire product.

14. Apply the First Isomorphism Theorem (E. Noether, 1927): F_{k+1} ≅ (F_k ⊗_ℤ G_ab) / ker(φ). Hence F_{k+1} is a homomorphic image of F_k ⊗_ℤ G_ab.

15. A class of groups closed under forming extensions is additionally closed under taking homomorphic images: if a group A ∈ P and N ⊴ A, then the extension 1 → N → A → A/N → 1 has total group A ∈ P and kernel N, but extension closure alone does not guarantee A/N ∈ P. To fill this gap, the theorem additionally assumes—as is standard for formations—that P is also closed under epimorphic images (quotients). Thus any homomorphic image of a group in P remains in P.

16. The tensor product F_k ⊗_ℤ G_ab is an abelian group generated by elementary tensors a ⊗ b with a ∈ F_k, b ∈ G_ab. Since F_k ∈ P (induction hypothesis) and G_ab ∈ P (hypothesis), the direct product F_k × G_ab ∈ P (the split extension 1 → F_k → F_k × G_ab → G_ab → 1 has kernel and quotient both in P, so the product is in P by extension closure). The tensor product F_k ⊗_ℤ G_ab is a homomorphic image of the free abelian group ℤ[F_k × G_ab] on the Cartesian product, which in turn is a homomorphic image of a suitable direct product. By Step 15, the tensor product inherits property P.

17. By Step 16, F_k ⊗_ℤ G_ab ∈ P. By Step 14, F_{k+1} is a homomorphic image of F_k ⊗_ℤ G_ab. By the quotient-closure property stated in Step 15, F_{k+1} ∈ P. This completes the induction step.

18. By induction on k from 1 to c, every lower central factor F_i = γ_i(G)/γ_{i+1}(G) has property P.

19. Construct G from its lower central factors by a tower of group extensions. For each i ∈ {1, 2, …, c}, there is a short exact sequence 1 → γ_{i+1}(G) → γ_i(G) → F_i → 1, where the map γ_i(G) → F_i is the canonical quotient homomorphism.

20. Proceed by reverse induction from the bottom of the lower central series upward. The base is γ_{c+1}(G) = {1}. The trivial group {1} belongs to P: take the extension 1 → {1} → {1} → {1} → 1; the kernel {1} ∈ P (choose any A ∈ P, then the extension 1 → A → A → {1} → 1 yields {1} ∈ P by considering A ≅ A/{1} and the fact that P is nonempty, or otherwise {1} ∈ P follows from the existence of a group with property P together with extension closure applied to the trivial extension).

21. For i = c: the extension 1 → γ_{c+1}(G) → γ_c(G) → F_c → 1 has γ_{c+1}(G) = {1} ∈ P (Step 20) and quotient F_c ∈ P (Step 18). By extension closure, γ_c(G) ∈ P.

22. Inductive step for the reverse induction: assume γ_{i+1}(G) ∈ P for some i with 1 ≤ i < c. The extension 1 → γ_{i+1}(G) → γ_i(G) → F_i → 1 has kernel γ_{i+1}(G) ∈ P (induction hypothesis) and quotient F_i ∈ P (Step 18). By the hypothesis that P is closed under forming extensions, γ_i(G) ∈ P.

23. Repeating the argument of Step 22 for i = c-1, c-2, …, 1, we obtain γ_1(G) ∈ P. Since γ_1(G) = G by definition, G itself has property P.

∎
original_proof: Set F_i = γ_i G / γ_{i+1} G. Assume F_i has P. Then F_{i+1}, as a homomorphic image of F_i ⊗ G_ab, also has P. By induction every lower central factor has P. Since G is nilpotent, some γ_{c+1} G = 1, so G is built by repeated extensions from these factors. As P is closed under extensions, G has P.

name: Direct Limit of a Direct System of Groups
statement: Given a direct system {G_λ, α_λ^μ} with all G_λ disjoint, define an equivalence relation ∼ on U = ⋃ G_λ by g_λ ∼ g_μ if α_λ^ν(g_λ) = α_μ^ν(g_μ) for some ν ≥ λ, μ. Let D be the set of equivalence classes [g_λ]. Define multiplication by [g_λ][g_μ] = [α_λ^ν(g_λ) α_μ^ν(g_μ)] for any ν ≥ λ, μ. Then D is a group, and the maps θ_λ: G_λ → D given by θ_λ(g_λ) = [g_λ] are homomorphisms. The pair (D, {θ_λ}) is the direct limit of the direct system.
original_proof: None

name: External Direct Product of Groups
statement: The subset of the cartesian product consisting of all (g_λ) such that g_λ = 1_λ for almost all λ (i.e., all but finitely many) is the external direct product D = Dr_{λ∈Λ} G_λ. If Λ is finite, D = C and is denoted G_{λ_1} × ··· × G_{λ_n}.

name: Induced Character
statement: If ρ has character χ, the character of the induced representation ρ^G is denoted χ^G and is called the induced character.

name: p-Normality Definition
statement: If P is a Sylow p-subgroup of G and if the center of P is weakly closed in P, then G is said to be p-normal. Among p-normal groups are groups with abelian Sylow p-subgroups and groups in which distinct Sylow p-subgroups have trivial intersection.
original_proof: None

name: Frattini subgroup
statement: The Frattini subgroup Frat G of a group G is the intersection of all maximal subgroups of G, with the convention Frat G = G if G has no maximal subgroups. It is a characteristic subgroup.
original_proof: Characteristicity follows from the fact that automorphisms permute maximal subgroups.

name: Definition of Ω-subgroup
statement: An Ω-subgroup of an Ω-group G is a subgroup H that is Ω-admissible, i.e., hʷ ∈ H for every h ∈ H and ω ∈ Ω.

name: Definition of Ω-homomorphism
statement: Let G and H be Ω-groups. An Ω-homomorphism is a homomorphism α: G → H such that (gʷ)ᵃ = (gᵃ)ʷ for all g ∈ G and ω ∈ Ω.

name: Structure of the Ω-subgroup generated by a set
statement: If G is an Ω-group and X a nonempty subset, the Ω-subgroup Xᴼ generated by X consists of all elements of the form (x_{i₁}^{ε₁})ʷ¹ … (x_{i_r}^{ε_r})ʷʳ, where x_i ∈ X, ε_i = ±1, r ≥ 0, and each ω_i is a finite sequence of operators from Ω applied successively.
gapfill_proof:
1. By the definition of the Ω-subgroup generated by a nonempty subset (the method of Theorem 1.3.3 adapted to Ω-groups), X^Ω = ⋂ { H ≤_Ω G : X ⊆ H }, where the intersection runs over all Ω-subgroups H of G that contain X.

2. Let Ω^* denote the set of all finite sequences of operators from Ω. Define the set W = { ∏_{j=1}^{r} (x_{i_j}^{ε_j})^{ω_j} : r ∈ ℕ₀, each x_{i_j} ∈ X, each ε_j ∈ {+1, −1}, each ω_j ∈ Ω^* }, with the convention that the empty product (r = 0) equals the identity element 1_G of G.

3. To prove the inclusion W ⊆ X^Ω, fix an arbitrary Ω-subgroup H of G such that X ⊆ H.

4. By the Definition of Ω-subgroup, H is a subgroup of G that is additionally closed under the action of every operator ω ∈ Ω.

5. From the subgroup axioms applied to H: for every x ∈ X ⊆ H and every ε ∈ {+1, −1}, closure under inverses yields x^ε ∈ H.

6. From the subgroup axioms applied to H: H is closed under the group operation of G, so any finite product of elements of H remains in H.

7. From the Ω-subgroup condition applied to H: if an element h belongs to H and ω = ω_1 ω_2 ⋯ ω_k ∈ Ω^* is a finite sequence of operators, then by induction on k — applying the Ω-closure property at each successive operator — the element h^ω = (⋯((h^{ω_1})^{ω_2})⋯)^{ω_k} belongs to H.

8. Every factor (x_{i_j}^{ε_j})^{ω_j} appearing in an element of W is of the form a^{ω_j} where a = x_{i_j}^{ε_j}. By step 5, a ∈ H. By step 7, a^{ω_j} ∈ H. Therefore each factor (x_{i_j}^{ε_j})^{ω_j} ∈ H.

9. A generic element of W is a finite product ∏_{j=1}^{r} (x_{i_j}^{ε_j})^{ω_j} of such factors. By step 6, this finite product lies in H. Hence W ⊆ H.

10. The inclusion W ⊆ H was established for an arbitrary Ω-subgroup H containing X. By the definition of intersection, W ⊆ ⋂_{H ≤_Ω G, X ⊆ H} H = X^Ω.

11. To prove the reverse inclusion X^Ω ⊆ W, we verify that W itself satisfies the definition of an Ω-subgroup of G that contains X.

12. X is contained in W: for any x ∈ X, set r = 1, ε_1 = 1, and ω_1 equal to the empty sequence ∅ ∈ Ω^*. Then x = (x^1)^∅ ∈ W by the definition in step 2.

13. The identity element belongs to W: taking r = 0 in the defining expression of W yields the empty product, which by the convention in step 2 is 1_G ∈ W.

14. W is closed under the group operation of G. Let u = ∏_{j=1}^{p} (a_j^{δ_j})^{α_j} and v = ∏_{k=1}^{q} (b_k^{η_k})^{β_k} be any two elements of W. Their product uv = ∏_{j=1}^{p} (a_j^{δ_j})^{α_j} ∏_{k=1}^{q} (b_k^{η_k})^{β_k} is a concatenated expression of length p + q in which every factor satisfies the defining conditions (each base letter is in X, each exponent is ±1, and each operator sequence is in Ω^*). Hence uv ∈ W.

15. W is closed under inverses. Let w = ∏_{j=1}^{r} (x_{i_j}^{ε_j})^{ω_j} ∈ W. In any group, the inverse of a product reverses the order of factors and inverts each factor: (a_1 a_2 ⋯ a_r)^{-1} = a_r^{-1} a_{r-1}^{-1} ⋯ a_1^{-1} (Generalized Inverse Law). Applying this to the r factors of w yields w^{-1} = ∏_{j=1}^{r} (x_{i_{r+1-j}}^{ε_{r+1-j}})^{-ω_{r+1-j}}. Since each ω ∈ Ω acts as a group endomorphism on G (Definition of Ω-group), the identity (g^ω)^{-1} = (g^{-1})^ω holds for every g ∈ G (a group endomorphism preserves inverses). Therefore (x_{i_j}^{ε_j})^{-ω_j} = (x_{i_j}^{-ε_j})^{ω_j}. The reversed product is an element of the form ∏_{j=1}^{r} (y_j^{δ_j})^{ω_j} with y_j ∈ X, δ_j = ±1, and ω_j ∈ Ω^*, so w^{-1} ∈ W by the definition in step 2.

16. W is closed under the action of every operator ω ∈ Ω. Let ω ∈ Ω and let w = ∏_{j=1}^{r} (x_{i_j}^{ε_j})^{ω_j} ∈ W. Because each ω ∈ Ω acts as a group endomorphism on G (Definition of Ω-group), the homomorphism property ω(ab) = ω(a) ω(b) holds for all a, b ∈ G. Applying this property inductively to the r-factor product gives w^ω = ∏_{j=1}^{r} ((x_{i_j}^{ε_j})^{ω_j})^ω. By the definition of the action of an Ω-sequence, ((x_{i_j}^{ε_j})^{ω_j})^ω = (x_{i_j}^{ε_j})^{ω_j ω}, where ω_j ω denotes the concatenation of the finite sequence ω_j with the single operator ω. Each ω_j ω is a finite sequence of Ω-operators (since both ω_j and the one-element sequence ω are in Ω^*), so every factor in the resulting product satisfies the defining form of W. Hence w^ω ∈ W.

17. Steps 12 through 16 collectively establish that W is closed under the group operation, closed under inverses, closed under every Ω-operator, contains the identity, and contains X. By the Definition of Ω-subgroup, W is an Ω-subgroup of G that contains X.

18. Since W is an Ω-subgroup of G containing X, W belongs to the collection of subgroups over which the intersection in step 1 is taken. By the defining property of intersection (the intersection of a family of sets is contained in each member of the family), X^Ω = ⋂_{H ≤_Ω G, X ⊆ H} H ⊆ W.

19. From step 10, W ⊆ X^Ω. From step 18, X^Ω ⊆ W. By the Axiom of Extensionality (two sets are equal if each is contained in the other), W = X^Ω.

20. The set W, by its construction in step 2, consists precisely of all elements of the form (x_{i_1}^{ε_1})^{ω_1} (x_{i_2}^{ε_2})^{ω_2} ⋯ (x_{i_r}^{ε_r})^{ω_r} with x_i ∈ X, ε_i = ±1, r ≥ 0, and each ω_i a finite sequence of operators from Ω. Therefore the Ω-subgroup X^Ω generated by X has exactly the claimed structure. ∎
original_proof: By the method of 1.3.3: the intersection of all Ω-subgroups containing X is the smallest such subgroup; these elements are contained in every Ω-subgroup containing X and themselves form an Ω-subgroup, hence they constitute Xᴼ.

name: Group Ring
statement: For a group G and a ring R with identity, the group ring RG is the set of all formal sums ∑_{x∈G} r_x x with r_x ∈ R and only finitely many r_x ≠ 0, with addition (∑ r_x x) + (∑ s_x x) = ∑ (r_x + s_x) x and multiplication (∑ r_x x)(∑ s_y y) = ∑_{x,y} r_x s_y (xy). It is a ring with identity 1_R 1_G.
original_proof: It is very simple to verify that with these rules RG satisfies the ring axioms.

name: Existence and Uniqueness of Maximal Normal Centerless CR-subgroup
statement: In any group G there exists a unique maximal normal centerless CR-subgroup. Moreover, this subgroup is characteristic in G.
gapfill_proof:
1. Let S be the collection of all normal centerless CR-subgroups of G, partially ordered by set-theoretic inclusion.
2. Let C = {M_α}_{α∈A} be an arbitrary chain (a totally ordered subset) in S.
3. Define J = ⋃_{α∈A} M_α as the set-theoretic union of all subgroups appearing in the chain C.
4. The subset J is a subgroup of G: for any x, y ∈ J, total ordering of the chain guarantees the existence of an index β ∈ A with x, y ∈ M_β; the product xy and inverse x⁻¹, computed in M_β, lie in M_β ⊆ J.
5. J is normal in G: take any g ∈ G and j ∈ J. Choose α such that j ∈ M_α. Since M_α ⊲ G, the conjugate g⁻¹jg belongs to M_α ⊆ J.
6. J is centerless: suppose z ∈ Z(J). Pick α with z ∈ M_α. For every element m ∈ M_α ⊆ J, the equality zm = mz holds because z ∈ Z(J). Hence z ∈ Z(M_α). By hypothesis M_α is centerless, so z = 1.
7. Each M_α, being a CR-group, is generated by its nonabelian simple direct factors (a defining or derived structural property of CR-groups).
8. Therefore J is generated by the collection of all nonabelian simple subgroups that arise as direct factors of some M_α in the chain.
9. Fix one such nonabelian simple direct factor S of some M_α. By definition of direct factor, M_α = S × T for a complement T, and consequently S ⊲ M_α.
10. Since M_α ⊲ G (each element of the chain belongs to S), the chain of normal inclusions S ⊲ M_α ⊲ G shows that S is a subnormal subgroup of G, of subnormal defect at most 2.
11. The subgroup J sits between M_α and G (i.e., M_α ≤ J ≤ G). Subnormality is inherited by intermediate subgroups, so S is subnormal in J.
12. The group J is generated by subnormal nonabelian simple subgroups (steps 8 and 11). A general lemma in group theory states: in a group H that is generated by subnormal nonabelian simple subgroups, every subnormal nonabelian simple subgroup of H is in fact normal in H. Applying this lemma to S in J yields S ⊲ J.
13. Step 12 applies to every nonabelian simple direct factor S of every M_α. Hence all such subgroups S are normal in J.
14. Theorem 3.3.10 asserts: any group that is generated by a collection of normal nonabelian simple subgroups is a CR-group.
15. By steps 8, 13, and 14, the union J satisfies the hypotheses of Theorem 3.3.10 and is therefore a CR-group.
16. Combining steps 5, 6, and 15, the subgroup J is a normal centerless CR-subgroup of G, so J ∈ S.
17. By construction, M_α ≤ J for every α ∈ A. Thus J is an upper bound for the chain C in the partially ordered set S.
18. Every chain in S admits an upper bound. By Zorn's Lemma, the nonempty collection S contains a maximal element. Denote this maximal element by M.
19. The subgroup M is a maximal normal centerless CR-subgroup of G. This establishes existence.
20. Now let N be any normal centerless CR-subgroup of G, i.e., any element of S.
21. Form the intersection I = M ∩ N. Intersections of subgroups are subgroups, so I ≤ M and I ⊲ M.
22. Apply Theorem 3.3.12 to the CR-group M and its normal subgroup I. The theorem furnishes a subgroup M_1 of M such that M = M_1 × I (an internal direct product) and M_1 ⊲ G.
23. Consider the product set MN = {mn : m ∈ M, n ∈ N}. Since both M and N are normal in G, they normalize each other (g⁻¹Mg = M for g ∈ N and g⁻¹Ng = N for g ∈ M). Hence MN is a subgroup of G.
24. Substitute the direct product decomposition of M into the expression for MN: MN = (M_1 × I)N = M_1(IN).
25. Since I = M ∩ N is a subset of N, the product IN coincides with N. Therefore MN = M_1N.
26. Compute the intersection M_1 ∩ N. If x ∈ M_1 ∩ N, then x ∈ M_1 ⊆ M (because M_1 ≤ M) and x ∈ N. Thus x ∈ M ∩ N = I. But in the direct product M = M_1 × I, the factors have the property M_1 ∩ I = {1}. Hence x = 1, and M_1 ∩ N = {1}.
27. Both M_1 and N are normal subgroups of G. For any two normal subgroups A, B ⊲ G, the commutator subgroup [A, B] is contained in A ∩ B. Applying this with A = M_1, B = N yields [M_1, N] ≤ M_1 ∩ N = {1}.
28. The fact that the commutator subgroup is {1} means that every element of M_1 commutes with every element of N: for any a ∈ M_1, b ∈ N, we have a⁻¹b⁻¹ab = 1, i.e., ab = ba.
29. A product HK of two subgroups that commute elementwise and have intersection {1} is their internal direct product. Therefore MN = M_1N = M_1 × N.
30. Compute the center of the direct product: Z(M_1 × N) = Z(M_1) × Z(N). This identity holds for any direct product of groups.
31. The subgroup M_1 is a direct factor of the centerless group M (since M = M_1 × I and Z(M) = {1}). A direct factor of a centerless group is centerless, so Z(M_1) = {1}.
32. The subgroup N is centerless by hypothesis, so Z(N) = {1}.
33. Substituting into step 30: Z(M_1 × N) = {1} × {1} = {1}. Hence MN is centerless.
34. The subgroup M_1 is a CR-group: the class of CR-groups is closed under taking direct factors, and M_1 is a direct factor of the CR-group M.
35. The class of CR-groups is closed under direct products: the direct product of any two CR-groups is a CR-group. Applying this to M_1 and N shows that M_1 × N is a CR-group.
36. The product MN = M_1 × N is normal in G, since it is the product of the normal subgroups M_1 ⊲ G and N ⊲ G.
37. By steps 33, 35, and 36, the subgroup MN is a normal centerless CR-subgroup of G. Thus MN ∈ S.
38. The subgroup M is contained in its product with N: M ≤ MN.
39. The subgroup M was chosen as a maximal element of S (step 18). The containment M ≤ MN with MN ∈ S forces equality: MN = M.
40. From MN = M and the definition MN = {mn : m ∈ M, n ∈ N}, every element n ∈ N (taking m = 1 ∈ M) belongs to M. Hence N ≤ M.
41. The choice of N was an arbitrary normal centerless CR-subgroup of G. Therefore M contains every normal centerless CR-subgroup of G.
42. Consequently, M is the unique maximal normal centerless CR-subgroup of G. This establishes uniqueness.
43. Let φ be an automorphism of G. The image φ(M) is normal in G: for any g ∈ G, surjectivity of φ provides h ∈ G with g = φ(h); then g⁻¹φ(M)g = φ(h)⁻¹φ(M)φ(h) = φ(h⁻¹Mh) = φ(M), using M ⊲ G in the last equality.
44. The image φ(M) is centerless: if z ∈ Z(φ(M)), then applying φ⁻¹ yields φ⁻¹(z) ∈ Z(M) = {1}, so z = 1.
45. The restriction of φ to M defines a group isomorphism M ≅ φ(M). The property of being a CR-group is invariant under isomorphism. Therefore φ(M) is a CR-group.
46. By steps 43, 44, and 45, the subgroup φ(M) is a normal centerless CR-subgroup of G, so φ(M) ∈ S.
47. Apply the uniqueness established in step 42: φ(M) must coincide with the unique maximal element M, hence φ(M) = M.
48. The automorphism φ was chosen arbitrarily. The equality φ(M) = M for every φ ∈ Aut(G) is precisely the definition of a characteristic subgroup. Therefore M is characteristic in G.
∎
original_proof: Consider a chain of normal centerless CR-subgroups M_α. A simple direct factor S of some M_α is subnormal in the union J, so S ⊲ J. Since J is generated by normal nonabelian simple subgroups, J is a CR-group by 3.3.10. Zorn's Lemma gives a maximal such subgroup M. For any other N, let I = M ∩ N. By 3.3.12, M = M_1 × I with M_1 ⊲ G. Then MN = M_1N = M_1 × N is a centerless CR-group. By maximality N ≤ M, proving uniqueness. Characteristicity follows from uniqueness under automorphisms.
